H=2: Red, Gray, Blue, Blue, Green, Gray, Yellow, Steel-Blue
H=3: Gray, Red, Blue, Gray, Yellow, Green, Steel-Blue, Gray
H=4: Orange, Gray, Green, Gray, Red, Blue, Orange, Gray
H=5: Steel-Blue, Yellow, Blue, Green, Blue, Orange, Teal, Red
H=6: Green, Gray, Yellow, Gray, Red, Gray, Steel-Blue, Blue
H=7: Yellow, Green, Blue, Orange, Gray, Red, Yellow, Steel-Blue
H=8: Orange, Blue, Red, Gray, Steel-Blue, Gray, Green, Yellow
H=9: Blue, Orange, Gray, Red, Teal, Gray, Steel-Blue, Green

SWIZZLE PATTERN ANALYSIS:
- The 128-byte swizzle mode permutes element indices within each row
- The permutation pattern shifts/XORs the column index based on the row index
- For H=0: sequential order 0,1,2,3,4,5,6,7 (no swap)
- For H=1: pairs swapped within groups of 2: (9,8),(11,10),(13,12),(15,14) — odd/even swap within pairs
- For H=2: groups of 4 rotated: (18,19,16,17),(22,23,20,21) — swap within groups of 4
- For H=3: groups of 8 reversed partially: 27,26,25,24,31,30,29,28 — reversal within groups of 4
- For H=4: 36,37,38,39,32,33,34,35 — upper/lower half of group of 8 swapped
- For H=5: 45,44,47,46,41,40,43,42 — combined swaps
- For H=6: 54,55,52,53,50,51,48,49 — further permutation
- For H=7: 63,62,61,60,59,58,57,56 — fully reversed within group of 8
- Pattern repeats with offset for H=8 and H=9

The swizzle XOR pattern per row H within a W-block follows: column_swizzled = column XOR (H mod 8), applied at the 128-byte (8-element) granularity, producing the observed permutations that avoid shared memory bank conflicts by ensuring different threads in a warp access different banks.
tensor-128B-swizzle-32B-atom.png:
FIGURE DESCRIPTION: 128-byte swizzle mode example with 32-byte atomicity

OVERVIEW:
Two grids are shown side by side vertically. The top grid is labeled "global memory layout" and the bottom grid is labeled "swizzled shared memory layout". Each grid is 4 rows by 8 columns, containing element indices 0 through 31.

DIMENSIONS:
- Both grids: 4 rows x 8 columns = 32 elements total
- Elements numbered 0 to 31
- Each row contains 8 elements
- 32-byte atomicity means each 2-element pair (or each cell) represents a 4-byte unit, so each row of 8 elements = 32 bytes
- Total tile = 4 rows x 32 bytes = 128 bytes (matching the 128-byte swizzle mode)

COLOR CODING (same colors used in both layouts, colors identify which global memory "chunk" each element belongs to):
- Yellow: elements 0, 4, 16, 20, 24, 28 (and others)
- Cyan/Blue: elements 1, 5, 9, 13, 17, 21, 25
- Red: elements 2, 10, 18, 26
- Gray: elements 3, 7, 11, 15, 19, 23, 27, 31
- Yellow (bright): elements 4, 12, 20, 28
- Purple/Lavender: elements 5, 13, 21, 29
- Green: elements 6, 14, 22, 30
- Gray (light): elements 7, 15, 23, 31

More precisely, the 8 colors correspond to 8 groups of 4 elements each, where each group occupies one column position in the global layout:
- Column 0 (yellow): 0, 8, 16, 24
- Column 1 (cyan): 1, 9, 17, 25
- Column 2 (red): 2, 10, 18, 26
- Column 3 (gray): 3, 11, 19, 27
- Column 4 (bright yellow): 4, 12, 20, 28
- Column 5 (purple): 5, 13, 21, 29
- Column 6 (green): 6, 14, 22, 30
- Column 7 (light gray): 7, 15, 23, 31

GLOBAL MEMORY LAYOUT (top grid):
Row 0: [0(yellow), 1(cyan), 2(red), 3(gray), 4(yellow), 5(purple), 6(green), 7(lt.gray)]
Row 1: [8(yellow), 9(cyan), 10(red), 11(gray), 12(yellow), 13(purple), 14(green), 15(lt.gray)]
Row 2: [16(yellow), 17(cyan), 18(red), 19(gray), 20(yellow), 21(purple), 22(green), 23(lt.gray)]
Row 3: [24(yellow), 25(cyan), 26(red), 27(gray), 28(yellow), 29(purple), 30(green), 31(lt.gray)]

Pattern: Sequential row-major order. Element index = row*8 + col.

SWIZZLED SHARED MEMORY LAYOUT (bottom grid):
Row 0: [0(yellow),  1(cyan),   2(red),    3(gray),   4(yellow),  5(purple),  6(green),   7(lt.gray)]
Row 1: [10(red),    11(gray),  8(yellow), 9(cyan),   14(green),  15(lt.gray),12(yellow), 13(purple)]
Row 2: [20(yellow), 21(purple),22(green), 23(lt.gray),16(yellow),17(cyan),   18(red),    19(gray)]
Row 3: [30(green),  31(lt.gray),28(yellow),29(purple),26(red),   27(gray),   24(yellow), 25(cyan)]

SWIZZLE PATTERN ANALYSIS:
The swizzle is a 128-byte swizzle with 32-byte atomicity. Each row is 32 bytes (8 elements x 4 bytes). The swizzle XORs the column index with the row index to remap positions.

Swizzle formula (column remapping per row):
- Row 0: col_swizzled = col XOR 0 → no change: columns [0,1,2,3,4,5,6,7]
- Row 1: col_swizzled = col XOR 2 (in units of 2-element pairs, i.e., XOR by 1 in pair-index):
  Positions map as: original col 0→col 2, col 1→col 3, col 2→col 0, col 3→col 1, col 4→col 6, col 5→col 7, col 6→col 4, col 7→col 5
  Result: [8→col2, 9→col3, 10→col0, 11→col1, 12→col6, 13→col7, 14→col4, 15→col5]
  Placed: [10, 11, 8, 9, 14, 15, 12, 13] ✓ matches
- Row 2: col_swizzled = col XOR 4 (shift by 4):
  col 0→4, 1→5, 2→6, 3→7, 4→0, 5→1, 6→2, 7→3
  Elements 16-23 placed at: [20,21,22,23,16,17,18,19] ✓ matches
- Row 3: col_swizzled = col XOR 6 (shift by 6, i.e., XOR 3 in pair units):
  col 0→6, 1→7, 2→4, 3→5, 4→2, 5→3, 6→0, 7→1
  Elements 24-31 placed at: [30,31,28,29,26,27,24,25] ✓ matches

SWIZZLE FORMULA SUMMARY:
swizzled_col = original_col XOR (row * 2)
where the XOR operates on column indices 0-7 and row*2 gives: row0→0, row1→2, row2→4, row3→6

BANK CONFLICT AVOIDANCE:
By XOR-ing the column with the row index, elements that would map to the same shared memory bank in different rows are spread across different banks. This ensures that a warp accessing one element per row (a column access pattern) hits different banks in each row, eliminating bank conflicts. The 32-byte atomicity means the swizzle granularity aligns with 32-byte boundaries, and the 128-byte total swizzle period covers the full 4-row tile.

SWIZZLE PATTERN ANALYSIS - Mapping from global position to shared memory position:

Row 0 is unchanged: elements 0-15 remain in columns 0-15.

Row 1 swizzle (global row 1 elements 16-31 placed into shared row 1):

Row 3 swizzle (global row 3 elements 48-63 placed into shared row 3):

- Shared col 14: element 51 (global col 3)
- Shared col 15: element 50 (global col 2)

SWIZZLE FORMULA DERIVATION:
The swizzle operates on the column index within each row. Let:
- row = row index (0-3)
- col = original column index (0-15)
- col' = swizzled column index

The 8-byte flip within 32-byte atomicity groups means:
- Within each 32-byte group (4 elements), adjacent pairs are swapped (XOR with 1 at the 8-byte level)
- The 32-byte group assignment itself is rotated based on the row number

For row r and column c (0-indexed), the swizzle can be expressed as:
- The column is divided into 32-byte groups of 4 elements each (groups 0-3 across 16 columns)
- The group index is XORed with the row index to determine which group maps where
- Within each group, the 8-byte flip reverses pairs

Specifically, the new column index col' for row r, original col c:
- group = c / 4 (integer division, groups 0-3)
- offset = c mod 4 (position within group, 0-3)
- new_group = group XOR row (XOR of group index with row index, mod 4)
- Within the group, the 8-byte flip swaps pairs: new_offset = offset XOR 1 for rows 1 and 3; no swap for rows 0 and 2 (or more precisely based on the pattern above)

Verification with row 1:
- col 0 (group 0, offset 0): new_group = 0 XOR 1 = 1, new_offset = 1 -> col' = 1*4+1 = 5 -> element 16+5=21 CHECK
- col 1 (group 0, offset 1): new_group = 1, new_offset = 0 -> col' = 4 -> element 20 CHECK
- col 4 (group 1, offset 0): new_group = 1 XOR 1 = 0, new_offset = 1 -> col' = 1 -> element 17 CHECK
- col 8 (group 2, offset 0): new_group = 2 XOR 1 = 3, new_offset = 1 -> col' = 13
tensor-128B-swizzle-64B-atom.png:
128-BYTE SWIZZLE MODE EXAMPLE WITH 64-BYTE ATOMICITY

FIGURE DESCRIPTION

This figure shows two memory layouts side by side (vertically stacked): a global memory layout (top) and a swizzled shared memory layout (bottom). Each layout is a 2-row by 8-column grid of numbered elements (0-15).

================================================================================
DIMENSIONS
================================================================================
- Grid size: 2 rows x 8 columns = 16 elements total
- Each row contains 8 elements
- Total elements: 16 (numbered 0 through 15)
- The 128-byte swizzle mode with 64-byte atomicity implies each element is 8 bytes (8 bytes x 16 elements = 128 bytes total), or each column group represents 16 bytes, with 8 columns x 16 bytes = 128 bytes per row.

================================================================================
GLOBAL MEMORY LAYOUT (top grid)
================================================================================
Row 0 (top row):    | 0  | 1  | 2  | 3  | 4  | 5  | 6  | 7  |
Row 1 (bottom row): | 8  | 9  | 10 | 11 | 12 | 13 | 14 | 15 |

Color coding in global memory layout:
Column 0: Yellow       (elements 0, 8)
Column 1: Blue/Cyan    (elements 1, 9)
Column 2: Red          (elements 2, 10)
Column 3: Gray         (elements 3, 11)
Column 4: Yellow       (elements 4, 12)
Column 5: Blue/Cyan    (elements 5, 13)
Column 6: Green        (elements 6, 14)
Column 7: Light Gray   (elements 7, 15)

In global memory, elements are stored in sequential row-major order:
Row 0: elements 0,1,2,3,4,5,6,7 at addresses [0..63 bytes] (first 64 bytes)
Row 1: elements 8,9,10,11,12,13,14,15 at addresses [64..127 bytes] (second 64 bytes)

================================================================================
SWIZZLED SHARED MEMORY LAYOUT (bottom grid)
================================================================================
Row 0 (top row):    | 0  | 1  | 2  | 3  | 4  | 5  | 6  | 7  |
Row 1 (bottom row): | 12 | 13 | 14 | 15 | 8  | 9  | 10 | 11 |

Color coding in swizzled shared memory layout:
Row 0:
  Column 0: Yellow       (element 0)
  Column 1: Blue/Cyan    (element 1)
  Column 2: Red          (element 2)
  Column 3: Gray         (element 3)
  Column 4: Yellow       (element 4)
  Column 5: Blue/Cyan    (element 5)
  Column 6: Green        (element 6)
  Column 7: Light Gray   (element 7)

Row 1:
  Column 0: Yellow       (element 12)
  Column 1: Light Purple/Lavender (element 13)
  Column 2: Dark Green   (element 14)
  Column 3: Light Gray   (element 15)
  Column 4: Orange/Yellow (element 8)
  Column 5: Blue/Cyan    (element 9)
  Column 6: Red          (element 10)
  Column 7: Dark Gray    (element 11)

================================================================================
SWIZZLE MAPPING (Global -> Shared)
================================================================================
Row 0 is unchanged:
  Global position (row=0, col=0..7) -> Shared position (row=0, col=0..7)
  Elements 0,1,2,3,4,5,6,7 remain in columns 0-7 of row 0.

Row 1 is swizzled - the two 64-byte halves are swapped relative to columns:
  Global row 1, columns 0-3 (elements 8,9,10,11) -> Shared row 1, columns 4-7
  Global row 1, columns 4-7 (elements 12,13,14,15) -> Shared row 1, columns 0-3

Explicit element mapping:
  Element 8  (global row1, col0) -> shared row1, col4
  Element 9  (global row1, col1) -> shared row1, col5
  Element 10 (global row1, col2) -> shared row1, col6
  Element 11 (global row1, col3) -> shared row1, col7
  Element 12 (global row1, col4) -> shared row1, col0
  Element 13 (global row1, col5) -> shared row1, col1
  Element 14 (global row1, col6) -> shared row1, col2
  Element 15 (global row1, col7) -> shared row1, col3

================================================================================
SWIZZLE PATTERN DESCRIPTION
================================================================================
Swizzle type: 128-byte swizzle with 64-byte atomicity.

The swizzle operates on the second row (row index 1) by XOR-ing the column index with a value derived from the row index and the 64-byte atomicity boundary:

For row 1: the second half (columns 4-7, i.e., the second 64-byte chunk) is swapped with the first half (columns 0-3, i.e., the first 64-byte chunk).

This is equivalent to: shared_col = global_col XOR 4  (for row 1 only)
  col 0 XOR 4 = 4
  col 1 XOR 4 = 5
  col 2 XOR 4 = 6
  col 3 XOR 4 = 7
  col 4 XOR 4 = 0
  col 5 XOR 4 = 1
  col 6 XOR 4 = 2
  col 7 XOR 4 = 3

Row 0 is unmodified (XOR with 0).

================================================================================
BANK CONFLICT AVOIDANCE
================================================================================
The swizzle ensures that when two rows are accessed simultaneously (e.g., in a warp accessing both rows), elements that would otherwise map to the same shared memory bank are now offset by 64 bytes (half the 128-byte row width). This prevents bank conflicts by ensuring that vertically aligned elements in the two rows land in different banks. The 64-byte atomicity means the swizzle granularity aligns with the 64-byte half-row boundary, rotating the second row's data by 4 column positions (64 bytes) relative to the first row.
cvt-rs-rbits-layout-f16.png:
LAYOUT TYPE: Bit field layout diagram showing how random bits (rbits) are organized for .rsr rounding with .f16 destination type. The figure shows a 32-bit value divided into four fields, with annotations indicating which portions are used for two separate inputs (a and b).

OVERALL STRUCTURE:
The diagram is a horizontal bit-field table with two labeled rows (Bits and User Input) and four columns representing contiguous bit ranges within a 32-bit rbits value.

DIMENSIONS AND BIT FIELDS:

The full 32-bit rbits value spans from bit 31 down to bit 0, divided as follows:

Column 1:
- Bits: 31...29 (3 bits wide)
- User Input: 0 (these bits are set to zero / unused)

Column 2:
- Bits: 28...16 (13 bits wide)
- User Input: Useful bits (these bits carry meaningful random data)

Column 3:
- Bits: 15...13 (3 bits wide)
- User Input: 0 (these bits are set to zero / unused)

Column 4:
- Bits: 12...0 (13 bits wide)
- User Input: Useful bits (these bits carry meaningful random data)

GROUPING AND ASSIGNMENT:

The 32-bit rbits value is split into two halves for two separate inputs:

rbits for a: covers bits 31...16 (upper 16 bits)
  - Bits 31...29: set to 0 (3 bits, unused/padding)
  - Bits 28...16: Useful bits (13 bits of actual random data for input a)

rbits for b: covers bits 15...0 (lower 16 bits)
  - Bits 15...13: set to 0 (3 bits, unused/padding)
  - Bits 12...0: Useful bits (13 bits of actual random data for input b)

PATTERN SUMMARY:
Each 16-bit half follows the same pattern: the top 3 bits are forced to 0, and the lower 13 bits contain the useful random bits. This pattern repeats symmetrically for both the upper half (input a) and lower half (input b) of the 32-bit rbits operand.

CAPTION TEXT BELOW DIAGRAM:
"Usage of rbits for different inputs"
cvt-rs-rbits-layout-bf16.png:
RANDOM BITS LAYOUT FOR .rsr ROUNDING WITH .bf16 DESTINATION TYPE

OVERVIEW:
This figure shows a 32-bit wide bit field layout describing how random bits (rbits) are organized and partitioned when performing stochastic rounding (.rsr) with a .bf16 destination type. The layout illustrates how a single 32-bit rbits value is split into two 16-bit halves, each serving as random bits for a separate input operand.

OVERALL STRUCTURE:
The figure contains a single horizontal register/field diagram that is 32 bits wide, divided into two equal 16-bit sections. There are two labeled rows: "Bits" (showing bit position ranges) and "User Input" (showing the content/purpose of each section).

BIT FIELD LAYOUT TABLE:

Row label: Bits
  Left cell:  bits 31......16  (upper 16 bits, most significant half)
  Right cell: bits 15......0   (lower 16 bits, least significant half)

Row label: User Input
  Left cell:  "Useful bits"    (occupying bit positions 31 down to 16)
  Right cell: "Useful bits"    (occupying bit positions 15 down to 0)

SPAN ANNOTATIONS (arrows shown below the table):

Top arrow: spans the entire 32-bit width (bits 31 to 0), labeled "rbits"
  Direction: left-to-right across the full width

Bottom annotations (two arrows side by side, spanning the full 32-bit width):
  Left arrow:  spans bits 31 to 16 (upper 16 bits), labeled "rbits for a"
  Right arrow: spans bits 15 to 0  (lower 16 bits), labeled "rbits for b"
  The two bottom arrows meet at the bit 16/15 boundary (center of the 32-bit field)

CAPTION BELOW DIAGRAM:
"Usage of rbits for different inputs"

KEY RELATIONSHIPS AND INTERPRETATION:
1. The full 32-bit rbits operand is provided by the user as a single value.
2. The upper 16 bits (bits 31 through 16) are designated as the random bits used for operand "a".
3. The lower 16 bits (bits 15 through 0) are designated as the random bits used for operand "b".
4. Both halves are labeled "Useful bits," indicating all 16 bits in each half are meaningful and used.
5. This partitioning allows a single 32-bit random value to simultaneously provide stochastic rounding bits for two separate .bf16 operands (a and b) in a single instruction.
6. The split is exactly at the midpoint: upper half for a, lower half for b, with no overlap or gap.

USAGE DESCRIPTION:
- Caption below the diagram reads: "Usage of rbits for different inputs"
- The 32-bit rbits input is split into two 16-bit halves
- Upper 16 bits (bits 31-16) are used as random bits for inputs a and b
- Lower 16 bits (bits 15-0) are used as random bits for inputs e and f
- Both halves are described as containing "Useful bits," meaning all 16 bits in each half are utilized for the stochastic rounding operation
- This layout applies uniformly to all four destination types: .e5m2x4, .e4m3x4, .e3m2x4, and .e2m3x4
mma-block-scaling.png:
CONCEPT BEING ILLUSTRATED:

This figure illustrates the matrix operand layout for the mma (matrix multiply-accumulate) instruction with block scaling using the scale_vec::2X modifier in NVIDIA PTX ISA. It shows how the input matrices A and B, their corresponding scale matrices (scale-A and scale-B), and the output/accumulator matrix C/D are organized and dimensioned relative to each other.

OVERALL LAYOUT:

The figure contains five distinct colored rectangular blocks arranged to suggest a matrix multiplication operation: D = A * B + C (or equivalently the output D replaces C). The arrangement follows the standard matrix multiplication layout where Matrix A is on the lower-left, Matrix B is on the upper-right, and Matrix C/D (accumulator/output) is on the lower-right. Scale matrices are positioned adjacent to their corresponding data matrices.

INDIVIDUAL COMPONENTS:

1. MATRIX A (green rectangle, lower-left):
   - Color: Green
   - Dimensions labeled: K (horizontal, width) and M (vertical, height)
   - The K dimension is indicated by a double-headed arrow spanning the full width of Matrix A, positioned above it
   - The M dimension is indicated by a double-headed arrow on the left side, spanning the full height
   - Matrix A is divided vertically into two halves by a vertical line through the middle (indicating the 2X block scaling subdivision along K)
   - Label: "Matrix A" appears below the rectangle

2. SCALE-A MATRIX (blue rectangle, immediately to the right of Matrix A):
   - Color: Blue
   - This is a narrow vertical rectangle positioned directly adjacent to the right edge of Matrix A
   - It has the same height (M dimension) as Matrix A
   - It appears to be divided into two narrow vertical columns (two thin blue strips side by side), corresponding to the 2X scaling (one scale value per half of K)
   - Label: "scale-A matrix" appears below the rectangle

3. MATRIX B (green rectangle, upper-right):
   - Color: Green
   - Dimensions labeled: N (horizontal, width) and K (vertical, height)
   - The N dimension is indicated by a double-headed arrow spanning the full width, positioned above Matrix B
   - The K dimension is indicated by a double-headed arrow on the left side of Matrix B, spanning the full height
   - Matrix B is divided horizontally into two halves by a horizontal line through the middle (indicating the 2X block scaling subdivision along K)
   - Label: "Matrix B" appears to the right of the rectangle

4. SCALE-B MATRIX (blue rectangle, below Matrix B):
   - Color: Blue
   - This is a wide, short horizontal rectangle positioned directly below Matrix B
   - It has the same width (N dimension) as Matrix B
   - It appears to consist of two thin horizontal rows stacked (two thin blue strips), corresponding to the 2X scaling (one scale value per half of K)
   - Label: "scale-B matrix" appears to the right of the rectangle

5. MATRIX C/D (yellow/orange rectangle, lower-right):
   - Color: Yellow/Orange
   - This represents both the input accumulator matrix C and the output matrix D
   - Dimensions correspond to M (height) x N (width), consistent with the result of an M x K times K x N multiplication
   - Label: "Matrix C/D" appears inside the rectangle
   - No internal subdivisions

DIMENSIONAL RELATIONSHIPS:

- Matrix A is M rows by K columns
- Matrix B is K rows by N columns
- Matrix C/D is M rows by N columns (the result)
- The K dimension is shared between Matrix A (width) and Matrix B (height), as expected in matrix multiplication
- The scale_vec::2X modifier means K is divided into 2 equal blocks, hence:
  - Matrix A is split into 2 vertical halves along K
  - Matrix B is split into 2 horizontal halves along K
  - scale-A matrix has 2 columns (one scale factor per K-block, per row of A), dimensions M x 2
  - scale-B matrix has 2 rows (one scale factor per K-block, per column of B), dimensions 2 x N

BLOCK SCALING INTERPRETATION:

The 2X in scale_vec::2X indicates that there are 2 scale vectors applied along the K dimension. Each half of K in Matrix A has a corresponding column in the scale-A matrix, and each half of K in Matrix B has a corresponding row in the scale-B matrix. This block scaling allows different scaling factors to be applied to different segments of the K dimension during the matrix multiply-accumulate operation.

DATA FLOW:

The computation proceeds as: D[m,n] = sum over k of (scale_A[m, k_block] * A[m,k] * scale_B[k_block, n] * B[k,n]) + C[m,n], where k_block indexes which of the 2 K-dimension blocks the element belongs to.
mma-scaling-thread-id-a-selection.png:
CONCEPT BEING ILLUSTRATED:
This figure illustrates how the thread-id-a value selects which set of scale factor values (SF_A) is used in a matrix multiply-accumulate (MMA) operation with M=16. It shows the mapping between thread IDs within quads and the 32-bit metadata registers for scale_A, demonstrating that thread-id-a selects a specific subset of threads (and their corresponding SF_A values) from the warp.

OVERALL LAYOUT:
The figure contains four grids arranged in a 2x2 layout. The left column (two grids stacked vertically) corresponds to thread-id-a = 1. The right column (two grids stacked vertically) corresponds to thread-id-a = 0. A vertical double-headed blue arrow runs between the left and right columns, labeled M=16, indicating the M dimension of the matrix operation is 16.

INDIVIDUAL GRID STRUCTURE:
Each of the four grids has the following structure:
- Horizontal span: labeled "32-bit metadata for scale_A" at the top (applies to both left and right columns)
- Each grid is divided into 4 columns (representing 4 elements within the 32-bit metadata) and 8 rows
- Rows are labeled Q0 through Q7 from bottom to top (Q0 at bottom, Q7 at top)
- Q0 through Q7 represent 8 quads (groups of 4 threads) within the warp

ROW COLOR CODING (same pattern in all four grids, from bottom Q0 to top Q7):
- Q0: dark brown/maroon
- Q1: light purple/lavender
- Q2: orange
- Q3: green
- Q4: light blue/cyan
- Q5: pink/magenta
- Q6: medium blue
- Q7: yellow

FOUR GRIDS AND THEIR LABELS:

Top-left grid:
- Label to the right: "Thread 2 from each quad"
- This grid is part of the thread-id-a = 1 selection

Bottom-left grid:
- Label to the right: "Thread 3 from each quad"
- This grid is also part of the thread-id-a = 1 selection

Top-right grid:
- Label to the right: "Thread 0 from each quad"
- This grid is part of the thread-id-a = 0 selection

Bottom-right grid:
- Label to the right: "Thread 1 from each quad"
- This grid is also part of the thread-id-a = 0 selection

BOTTOM ANNOTATIONS:
- Below the left column (two grids): "thread-id-a = 1 selects these values for SF_A"
- Below the right column (two grids): "thread-id-a = 0 selects these values for SF_A"
- Between the two columns at the bottom: "M=16"

DATA FLOW AND SELECTION LOGIC:
- When thread-id-a = 0: Threads 0 and 1 from each quad (Q0-Q7) are selected to provide SF_A values. These correspond to the two grids on the right side (top-right: Thread 0 from each quad; bottom-right: Thread 1 from each quad).
- When thread-id-a = 1: Threads 2 and 3 from each quad (Q0-Q7) are selected to provide SF_A values. These correspond to the two grids on the left side (top-left: Thread 2 from each quad; bottom-left: Thread 3 from each quad).
- Each quad contains 4 threads (threads 0, 1, 2, 3), and the thread-id-a bit selects which pair of threads within each quad contributes the scale factor metadata.
- The 32-bit metadata for scale_A is divided into 4 columns within each grid, representing the 4 data elements packed into the 32-bit register.
- There are 8 quads (Q0-Q7) across the warp, each contributing one row of scale factor data.
- The M=16 label with the vertical arrow indicates this layout covers 16 rows of the A matrix (8 quads x 2 rows per quad = 16 rows).

NUMERICAL VALUES:
- M = 16 (matrix M dimension)
- 32 bits wide for scale_A metadata
- 8 quads labeled Q0 through Q7
- 4 threads per quad (threads 0, 1, 2, 3)
- 4 columns per grid (4 elements in the 32-bit metadata)
- thread-id-a values: 0 or 1 (1-bit selector)
mma-scaling-thread-id-b-selection.png:
CONCEPT BEING ILLUSTRATED:
This figure shows how the scale factor set SF_B is selected from a 32-bit metadata register based on the thread-id-b value (0, 1, 2, or 3) within a quad. It illustrates the mapping between thread identity within a quad and which row of scale factor values that thread is responsible for selecting from the scale_B metadata.

OVERALL LAYOUT:
There are four grids arranged in a 2x2 layout, one for each possible thread-id-b value (0, 1, 2, 3). Each grid represents the same 32-bit metadata structure for scale_B, but with a different row highlighted to indicate which values are selected by that particular thread.

GRID STRUCTURE (same for all four panels):
Each grid is 4 rows tall by 8 columns wide.
The horizontal dimension is labeled N=8, spanning columns Q0 through Q7 (labeled left to right as Q7, Q6, Q5, Q4, Q3, Q2, Q1, Q0 — i.e., Q7 is on the far left and Q0 is on the far right).
The vertical dimension (4 rows) corresponds to the 32-bit metadata for scale_B, as indicated by a double-headed vertical arrow between the top-left and top-right panels labeled "32-bit metadata for scale_B".

COLUMN LABELS (left to right as shown):
Q7, Q6, Q5, Q4, Q3, Q2, Q1, Q0

COLOR CODING OF COLUMNS (consistent across all panels):
Q7: Yellow
Q6: Light blue (cyan)
Q5: Magenta/pink-purple
Q4: Light blue (slightly different shade, or same cyan)
Q3: Green
Q2: Orange
Q1: Light pink/lavender
Q0: Dark red/brown

Each column has a consistent color across all 4 rows within a grid.

HIGHLIGHTED ROW PER PANEL:
Each panel highlights one specific row (the row selected by that thread-id-b), while the other rows appear in the same colors but are not the selected set.

Note: The visual distinction between the selected row and non-selected rows is through the same color scheme — the figure uses color to identify columns (Q0–Q7), and the row position indicates which thread selects it.

FOUR PANELS IN DETAIL:

TOP-LEFT PANEL:
Label: "Thread 0 from each quad"
Sub-label: "thread-id-b = 0 selects these values for SF_B"
N=8 arrow spans the top.
Grid: 4 rows x 8 columns (Q7 to Q0).
Thread 0 selects the top row (row 0) of the scale_B metadata.

TOP-RIGHT PANEL:
Label: "Thread 1 from each quad"
Sub-label: "thread-id-b = 1 selects these values for SF_B"
N=8 arrow spans the top.
Grid: 4 rows x 8 columns (Q7 to Q0).
Thread 1 selects the second row (row 1) of the scale_B metadata.

BOTTOM-LEFT PANEL:
Label: "Thread 2 from each quad"
Sub-label: "thread-id-b = 2 selects these values for SF_B"
N=8 arrow spans the top.
Grid: 4 rows x 8 columns (Q7 to Q0).
Thread 2 selects the third row (row 2) of the scale_B metadata.

BOTTOM-RIGHT PANEL:
Label: "Thread 3 from each quad"
Sub-label: "thread-id-b = 3 selects these values for SF_B"
N=8 arrow spans the top.
Grid: 4 rows x 8 columns (Q7 to Q0).
Thread 3 selects the fourth (bottom) row (row 3) of the scale_B metadata.

NUMERICAL VALUES AND DIMENSIONS:
N = 8 (number of columns, representing 8 scale factor values per row)
4 rows total in each grid (corresponding to 4 threads per quad, thread-id-b = 0, 1, 2, 3)
32-bit metadata total for scale_B (4 rows x 8 columns = 32 entries, each presumably 1 bit, or the 32-bit register encodes all scale factors)

DATA FLOW AND ORGANIZATION:
The 32-bit metadata register for scale_B is organized as a 4-row by 8-column structure.
Each row corresponds to one thread within a quad (thread-id-b 0 through 3).
A thread with thread-id-b = k selects row k from the metadata, obtaining 8 scale factor values (one per column Q0–Q7) for SF_B.
This selection mechanism allows each of the 4 threads in a quad to access a distinct set of 8 scale factors from the shared 32-bit metadata.

ARROWS AND ANNOTATIONS:
Horizontal double-headed arrow above each grid labeled "N=8" indicating the width dimension.
Vertical double-headed arrow between the two top panels labeled "32-bit metadata for scale_B" indicating the height/depth of the metadata structure corresponds to 4 rows (one per thread in a quad).
mma-scaling-byte-id-selection.png:
CONCEPT BEING ILLUSTRATED

This figure illustrates how a byte-id value (byte-id-a or byte-id-b) selects one or more scale factor values (SF_A or SF_B) from a 32-bit metadata register, depending on the scale_vec mode (.scale_vec::1X, .scale_vec::2X, or .scale_vec::4X). The selected scale factors are highlighted in green in each diagram.

OVERALL STRUCTURE

There are 6 sub-diagrams arranged in a 2-column, 3-row grid. Each sub-diagram shows a 32-bit register divided into 4 equal fields (SF3, SF2, SF1, SF0), with bit positions labeled at the top (31 on the left, 15 in the middle, 0 on the right). Selected fields are shown in green (highlighted); unselected fields are shown in gray/white.

REGISTER LAYOUT (common to all diagrams)

The 32-bit metadata register is divided into 4 scale factor fields, each 8 bits wide:
  Bits 31-24: SF3 (leftmost field)
  Bits 23-16: SF2
  Bits 15-8:  SF1
  Bits 7-0:   SF0 (rightmost field)

Bit position markers shown above each register: 31 (far left), 15 (middle), 0 (far right).

INDIVIDUAL SUB-DIAGRAMS (left column, top to bottom)

Diagram 1 (top-left):
  All four fields (SF3, SF2, SF1, SF0) are shown in gray (none highlighted/selected).
  Caption: "32-bit metadata register contains 4 scale values"
  This is the baseline/reference diagram showing the structure of the metadata register.

Diagram 2 (middle-left):
  SF3 is highlighted green; SF2, SF1, SF0 are gray.
  Caption: "Byte-id of 3 for .scale_vec::1X selects SF3"
  Meaning: When scale_vec mode is 1X and byte-id = 3, only SF3 is selected.

Diagram 3 (bottom-left):
  SF3 and SF2 are highlighted green; SF1 and SF0 are gray.
  Caption: "Byte-id of 2 for .scale_vec::2X selects SF2 and SF3"
  Meaning: When scale_vec mode is 2X and byte-id = 2, both SF2 and SF3 are selected.

INDIVIDUAL SUB-DIAGRAMS (right column, top to bottom)

Diagram 4 (top-right):
  SF1 is highlighted green; SF3, SF2, SF0 are gray.
  Caption: "Byte-id of 1 for .scale_vec::1X selects SF1"
  Meaning: When scale_vec mode is 1X and byte-id = 1, only SF1 is selected.

Diagram 5 (middle-right):
  SF1 and SF0 are highlighted green; SF3 and SF2 are gray.
  Caption: "Byte-id of 0 for .scale_vec::2X selects SF0 and SF1"
  Meaning: When scale_vec mode is 2X and byte-id = 0, both SF0 and SF1 are selected.

Diagram 6 (bottom-right):
  All four fields (SF3, SF2, SF1, SF0) are highlighted green.
  Caption: "Byte-id must be 0 for .scale_vec::4X"
  Meaning: When scale_vec mode is 4X, byte-id must be 0, and all four scale factors (SF0, SF1, SF2, SF3) are selected simultaneously.

SELECTION RULES SUMMARIZED FROM THE FIGURE

.scale_vec::1X mode: byte-id selects a single scale factor field.
  byte-id = 0 would select SF0 (not shown but implied by pattern)
  byte-id = 1 selects SF1
  byte-id = 2 would select SF2 (not shown but implied)
  byte-id = 3 selects SF3

.scale_vec::2X mode: byte-id selects a pair of adjacent scale factor fields.
  byte-id = 0 selects SF0 and SF1 (lower pair)
  byte-id = 2 selects SF2 and SF3 (upper pair)

.scale_vec::4X mode: byte-id must be 0, and all four scale factors (SF0, SF1, SF2, SF3) are selected.

DATA FLOW / ORGANIZATION PATTERN

The byte-id acts as an index into the 32-bit metadata register. The scale_vec mode determines the granularity of selection: 1X selects 1 field (8 bits), 2X selects 2 fields (16 bits), 4X selects all 4 fields (32 bits). Higher scale_vec values select more scale factors simultaneously and restrict valid byte-id values accordingly.
mma-884-A-col-f16.png:
MMA .m8n8k4 Fragment Layout for Column-Major Matrix A with .f16 Type

===========================================================================

1. MATRIX IDENTIFICATION
------------------------
Operation: MMA .m8n8k4
Matrix: A (input matrix)
Data type: .f16 (16-bit floating point)
Matrix dimensions: M=8, N=8, K=4
Layout order: Column-major

===========================================================================

2. OVERALL STRUCTURE
--------------------
The layout is divided into 4 MMA computations (MMA computation 1 through 4).
Each MMA computation covers a subset of threads and a subset of rows.
The grid uses R\C notation where R = row index (0-7) and C = column index (0..3).
The columns represent the K dimension (columns 0 through 3).

===========================================================================

3. REGISTER ELEMENT PACKING
-----------------------------
Each thread holds 4 elements: a0, a1, a2, a3.
Since the data type is .f16 (16-bit), two f16 values are packed into each 32-bit register.
Therefore: 4 elements = 2 registers (each 32-bit register holds 2 f16 values).
  - Register 0: {a0, a1} (a0 in low 16 bits, a1 in high 16 bits)
  - Register 1: {a2, a3} (a2 in low 16 bits, a3 in high 16 bits)

===========================================================================

4. DETAILED THREAD-TO-ELEMENT MAPPING BY MMA COMPUTATION
----------------------------------------------------------

MMA COMPUTATION 1
  Rows covered: 0, 1, 2, 3 (upper half) and 4, 5, 6, 7 (lower half)
  Columns covered: 0 .. 3

  Upper rows (R = 0, 1, 2, 3):
    Thread T0  (column 0): holds {a0, a1, a2, a3}
    Threads T1, T2 (columns 1, 2): implied by ".." (same pattern, intermediate threads)
    Thread T3  (column 3): holds {a0, a1, a2, a3}
    Arrow from T0 to T3 indicates T0 through T3 span columns 0 through 3.

  Lower rows (R = 4, 5, 6, 7):
    Thread T16 (column 0): holds {a0, a1, a2, a3}
    Threads T17, T18 (columns 1, 2): implied by ".." (same pattern)
    Thread T19 (column 3): holds {a0, a1, a2, a3}
    Arrow from T16 to T19 indicates T16 through T19 span columns 0 through 3.

MMA COMPUTATION 2
  Rows covered: 0, 1, 2, 3 (upper half) and 4, 5, 6, 7 (lower half)
  Columns covered: 0 .. 3

  Upper rows (R = 0, 1, 2, 3):
    Thread T4  (column 0): holds {a0, a1, a2, a3}
    Threads T5, T6 (columns 1, 2): implied by ".."
    Thread T7  (column 3): holds {a0, a1, a2, a3}
    Arrow from T4 to T7.

  Lower rows (R = 4, 5, 6, 7):
    Thread T20 (column 0): holds {a0, a1, a2, a3}
    Threads T21, T22 (columns 1, 2): implied by ".."
    Thread T23 (column 3): holds {a0, a1, a2, a3}
    Arrow from T20 to T23.

MMA COMPUTATION 3
  Rows covered: 0, 1, 2, 3 (upper half) and 4, 5, 6, 7 (lower half)
  Columns covered: 0 .. 3

  Upper rows (R = 0, 1, 2, 3):
    Thread T8  (column 0): holds {a0, a1, a2, a3}
    Threads T9, T10 (columns 1, 2): implied by ".."
    Thread T11 (column 3): holds {a0, a1, a2, a3}
    Arrow from T8 to T11.

  Lower rows (R = 4, 5, 6, 7):
    Thread T24 (column 0): holds {a0, a1, a2, a3}
    Threads T25, T26 (columns 1, 2): implied by ".."
    Thread T27 (column 3): holds {a0, a1, a2, a3}
    Arrow from T24 to T27.

MMA COMPUTATION 4
  Rows covered: 0, 1, 2, 3 (upper half) and 4, 5, 6, 7 (lower half)
  Columns covered: 0 .. 3

  Upper rows (R = 0, 1, 2, 3):
    Thread T12 (column 0): holds {a0, a1, a2, a3}
    Threads T13, T14 (columns 1, 2): implied by ".."
    Thread T15 (column 3): holds {a0, a1, a2, a3}
    Arrow from T12 to T15.

  Lower rows (R = 4, 5, 6, 7):
    Thread T28 (column 0): holds {a0, a1, a2, a3}
    Threads T29, T30 (columns 1, 2): implied by ".."
    Thread T31 (column 3): holds {a0, a1, a2, a3}
    Arrow from T28 to T31.

===========================================================================

5. COMPLETE THREAD GROUPING SUMMARY (all 32 threads)
------------------------------------------------------

Group 1 (MMA computation 1, rows 0-3):   T0,  T1,  T2,  T3   -> columns 0,1,2,3
Group 2 (MMA computation 2, rows 0-3):   T4,  T5,  T6,  T7   -> columns 0,1,2,3
Group 3 (MMA computation 3, rows 0-3):   T8,  T9,  T10, T11  -> columns 0,1,2,3
Group 4 (MMA computation 4, rows 0-3):   T12, T13, T14, T15  -> columns 0,1,2,3
Group 5 (MMA computation 1, rows 4-7):   T16, T17, T18, T19  -> columns 0,1,2,3
Group 6 (MMA computation 2, rows 4-7):   T20, T21, T22, T23  -> columns 0,1,2,3
Group 7 (MMA computation 3, rows 4-7):   T24, T25, T26, T27  -> columns 0,1,2,3
Group 8 (MMA computation 4, rows 4-7):   T28, T29, T30, T31  -> columns 0,1,2,3

Within each group, each thread holds the same named elements {a0, a1, a2, a3},
corresponding to 4 consecutive K-dimension elements for that thread's assigned row.

===========================================================================

6. ANNOTATION AT BOTTOM
-------------------------
Label: %laneid:{fragments}
This indicates the mapping is indexed by lane ID within the warp, and each lane
holds the fragment elements as described above.

===========================================================================

7. KEY OBSERVATIONS
--------------------
- All 32 threads (T0-T31) participate in the matrix A fragment.
- Each thread holds exactly 4 f16 elements (a0, a1, a2, a3) packed into 2 x 32-bit registers.
- The 4 MMA computations partition the 32 threads into groups of 4 consecutive threads per computation per row-band.
- Threads T0-T15 cover rows 0-3; threads T16-T31 cover rows 4-7.
- Within each MMA computation, 4 threads (one per K column 0-3) hold the same element labels, meaning each thread covers one column of the K=4 dimension for its assigned row.
- The arrow notation (e.g., T0 -> T3) indicates that threads T0 through T3 are consecutive and each maps to one of the 4 K-columns (0, 1, 2, 3) respectively.

T2:  (col2, same pattern)
