=== THREAD ASSIGNMENT PATTERN ===
Thread ID within each column group follows this pattern:
  Column 0: T0, T1, T2, T3  (for row groups 32-35, 36-39, 40-43, 44-47 respectively)
  Column 1: T4, T5, T6, T7
  Column 2: T8, T9, T10, T11
  Column 3: T12, T13, T14, T15
  Column 4: T16, T17, T18, T19
  Column 5: T20, T21, T22, T23
  Column 6: T24, T25, T26, T27

  where row_group_index: 0=rows32-35, 1=rows36-39, 2=rows40-43, 3=rows44-47

=== REGISTER ELEMENT DETAILS ===

For 8-bit data types (u8/s8) or floating point types (e4m3/e5m2/e3m2/e2m3/e2m1):
  Multiple 8-bit elements are packed into each 32-bit register
  b8 and b9 are packed into one 32-bit register
  b10 and b11 are packed into one 32-bit register
  (4 elements total per thread = 2 registers of 32 bits each, with 2 elements per register for 8-bit types)

=== TOTAL THREAD COVERAGE ===
All 32 threads (T0-T31) are used
Each thread covers one specific (row-group, column) intersection
32 threads x 4 elements = 128 element slots covering the 16-row x 8-column sparse B fragment for rows 32-47

Coverage: Columns 0 through 31 (first half of k=64 dimension)

TABLE STRUCTURE
The table has rows labeled 0–15 (matrix rows) and columns labeled by 4-wide column groups: 0..3, 4..7, 8..11, 12..15, 16..19, 20..23, 24..27, 28..31.

Each cell contains: Thread identifier (T_n) and bit range within that thread's metadata register, in the format T_n: [high..low].

FULL CELL-BY-CELL DATA

Row 0:

  Cols 4..7:   T0: [7..4]

  Cols 8..11:  T4: [11..8]
  Cols 12..15: T4: [15..12]
  Cols 16..19: T4: [19..16]
  Cols 20..23: T4: [23..20]
  Cols 24..27: T4: [27..24]
  Cols 28..31: T4: [31..28]

Row 2: (cells not explicitly labeled — implied continuation of pattern, indicated by diagonal arrow from row 1 col 28..31 toward row 7 col 0..3)

Row ... (rows 2–6 follow the same stride pattern, not individually labeled)

Row 7:
  Cols 0..3:   T28: [3..0]
  Cols 4..7:   T28: [7..4]
  Cols 8..11:  T28: [11..8]
  Cols 12..15: T28: [15..12]
  Cols 16..19: T28: [19..16]
  Cols 20..23: T28: [23..20]
  Cols 24..27: T28: [27..24]
  Cols 28..31: T28: [31..28]

Row 8:
  Cols 0..3:   T1: [3..0]
  Cols 4..7:   T1: [7..4]
  Cols 8..11:  T1: [11..8]
  Cols 12..15: T1: [15..12]
  Cols 16..19: T1: [19..16]
  Cols 20..23: T1: [23..20]
  Cols 24..27: T1: [27..24]
  Cols 28..31: T1: [31..28]

Row 9:
  Cols 0..3:   T5: [3..0]
  Cols 4..7:   T5: [7..4]
  Cols 8..11:  T5: [11..8]
  Cols 12..15: T5: [15..12]
  Cols 16..19: T5: [19..16]
  Cols 20..23: T5: [23..20]
  Cols 24..27: T5: [27..24]
  Cols 28..31: T5: [31..28]

Row 10: (cells not explicitly labeled — implied continuation, indicated by diagonal arrow from row 9 col 28..31 toward row 15 col 0..3)

Row ... (rows 10–14 follow the same stride pattern, not individually labeled)

Row 15:
  Cols 0..3:   T29: [3..0]
  Cols 4..7:   T29: [7..4]
  Cols 8..11:  T29: [11..8]
  Cols 12..15: T29: [15..12]
  Cols 16..19: T29: [19..16]
  Cols 20..23: T29: [23..20]
  Cols 24..27: T29: [27..24]
  Cols 28..31: T29: [31..28]

THREAD ASSIGNMENT PATTERN

Rows 0–7 use threads T0, T4, T8, T12, T16, T20, T24, T28 (stride of +4 per row).
  Row 0 -> T0
  Row 1 -> T4
  Row 2 -> T8
  Row 3 -> T12
  Row 4 -> T16
  Row 5 -> T20
  Row 6 -> T24
  Row 7 -> T28

Rows 8–15 use threads T1, T5, T9, T13, T17, T21, T25, T29 (stride of +4 per row, offset +1 from upper half).
  Row 8  -> T1
  Row 9  -> T5
  Row 10 -> T9
  Row 11 -> T13
  Row 12 -> T17
  Row 13 -> T21
  Row 14 -> T25
  Row 15 -> T29

BIT FIELD ASSIGNMENT PATTERN

Each thread holds a single 32-bit metadata register covering all 8 column groups (cols 0..31).
Each group of 4 columns consumes exactly 4 bits of the metadata register.
The bit fields are contiguous and non-overlapping across the 32-bit register:

  Cols 0..3:   bits [3..0]
  Cols 4..7:   bits [7..4]
  Cols 8..11:  bits [11..8]
  Cols 12..15: bits [15..12]
  Cols 16..19: bits [19..16]
  Cols 20..23: bits [23..20]
  Cols 24..27: bits [27..24]
  Cols 28..31: bits [31..28]

This means each 4-bit field encodes the sparsity selector for 4 consecutive columns within one matrix row. For 2:4 structured sparsity, each group of 4 elements contains exactly 2 non-zeros; 4 bits encode two 2-bit selectors (each 2-bit value selects which of the 4 positions is non-zero).

DIAGONAL ARROWS
Two diagonal arrows are shown in the figure:
1. An arrow from the row 1 / cols 28..31 cell (T4: [31..28]) pointing diagonally down-left toward row 7 / cols 0..3 (T28: [3..0]), indicating the continuation of the thread sequence T4 -> T8 -> ... -> T28 across rows 2–7.
2. An arrow from the row 9 / cols 28..31 cell (T5: [31..28]) pointing diagonally down-left toward row 15 / cols 0..3 (T29: [3..0]), indicating the continuation of the thread sequence T5 -> T9 -> ... -> T29 across rows 10–15.

SUMMARY OF KEY STRUCTURAL RULES
1. Each matrix row maps to exactly one thread.
2. Each thread's 32-bit register covers all 32 columns (cols 0..31) for that row.
3. Bit fields are 4 bits wide per 4-column group, packed LSB to MSB left to right.
4. Upper 8 rows (0–7) use even-indexed threads in multiples of 4: T0, T4, T8, T12, T16, T20, T24, T28.
5. Lower 8 rows (8–15) use odd-indexed threads offset by 1: T1, T5, T9, T13, T17, T21, T25, T29.
6. Threads T2, T3, T6, T7, T10, T11, etc. (those with index mod 4 = 2 or 3) are not used for this metadata layout in columns 0–31 (they likely cover columns 32–63, the second half of k=64).

Row 9:
  Cols 35..32:  T7:  [3..0]
  Cols 39..36:  T7:  [7..4]
  Cols 43..40:  T7:  [11..8]
  Cols 47..44:  T7:  [15..12]
  Cols 51..48:  T7:  [19..16]
  Cols 55..52:  T7:  [23..20]
  Cols 59..56:  T7:  [27..24]
  Cols 63..60:  T7:  [31..28]

Row 10: (empty cells — implied continuation of pattern)

Row 11 through 14: indicated by "..." (pattern continues)

Row 15:
  Cols 35..32:  T31: [3..0]
  Cols 39..36:  T31: [7..4]
  Cols 43..40:  T31: [11..8]
  Cols 47..44:  T31: [15..12]
  Cols 51..48:  T31: [19..16]
  Cols 55..52:  T31: [23..20]
  Cols 59..56:  T31: [27..24]
  Cols 63..60:  T31: [31..28]

THREAD PROGRESSION PATTERN:
Rows 0-7 use threads: T2, T6, ..., T30 (even-numbered threads starting at 2, incrementing by 4: T2, T6, T10, T14, T18, T22, T26, T30)
Rows 8-15 use threads: T3, T7, ..., T31 (odd-numbered threads starting at 3, incrementing by 4: T3, T7, T11, T15, T19, T23, T27, T31)

BIT ALLOCATION PER THREAD:
Each thread holds a 32-bit metadata register [31..0].
The 32 bits are divided into 8 groups of 4 bits each:
  Bits [3..0]   -> column group 35..32
  Bits [7..4]   -> column group 39..36
  Bits [11..8]  -> column group 43..40
  Bits [15..12] -> column group 47..44
  Bits [19..16] -> column group 51..48
  Bits [23..20] -> column group 55..52
  Bits [27..24] -> column group 59..56
  Bits [31..28] -> column group 63..60

ENCODING FORMAT:
Each 4-bit field encodes sparsity metadata for 4 consecutive columns. In 2:4 structured sparsity, 2 non-zero elements are selected out of every 4 elements. Each 4-bit group encodes which 2 of the 4 elements in that column group are non-zero (2 bits per selected element index).

DIAGONAL ARROWS:
Two diagonal arrows are visible in the figure, indicating the reading/continuation direction of the thread pattern across rows 2-7 (upper arrow) and rows 10-15 (lower arrow), showing that intermediate rows follow the same incremental thread numbering pattern between the explicitly shown boundary rows.

RELATIONSHIP TO COLUMNS 0-31:
This figure covers columns 32-63 only. The companion figure for columns 0-31 covers the first half of the k=64 dimension. Threads T2/T6/.../T30 and T3/T7/.../T31 each hold the full 32-bit metadata for both halves of their assigned rows, with bits [31..0] covering columns 32-63 as shown here.

ROW 15, Columns 32-47: T30:{a8 ... a15}
ROW 15, Columns 48-63: T31:{a8 ... a15}

==============================================================
4. GROUPING PATTERNS
==============================================================
THREAD GROUPING BY ROW BLOCK:
- Matrix rows 0-7:  Threads T0-T31, each thread holds elements a0...a7
  - Row 0: T0, T1, T2, T3 (columns 0-15, 16-31, 32-47, 48-63)
  - Row 1: T4, T5, T6, T7
  - Row 2: T8, T9, T10, T11
  - Row 3: T12, T13, T14, T15
  - Row 4: T16, T17, T18, T19
  - Row 5: T20, T21, T22, T23
  - Row 6: T24, T25, T26, T27
  - Row 7: T28, T29, T30, T31

- Matrix rows 8-15: Threads T0-T31 REUSED, each thread holds elements a8...a15
  - Row 8:  T0, T1, T2, T3
  - Row 9:  T4, T5, T6, T7
  - Row 10: T8, T9, T10, T11
  - Row 11: T12, T13, T14, T15
  - Row 12: T16, T17, T18, T19
  - Row 13: T20, T21, T22, T23
  - Row 14: T24, T25, T26, T27
  - Row 15: T28, T29, T30, T31

COLUMN GROUPING:
- Each thread covers a 16-column span of the K dimension
- 4 threads per row, each covering 16 columns: columns 0-15, 16-31, 32-47, 48-63

==============================================================
5. REGISTER ELEMENT PACKING
==============================================================
Total elements per thread: 16 elements (a0 through a15)
Split into two groups:
- a0...a7:  8 elements, used for matrix rows 0-7
- a8...a15: 8 elements, used for matrix rows 8-15

Since the data type is 4-bit (.u4 or .s4):
- Each 32-bit register holds 8 x 4-bit values
- Therefore a0...a7 fits in 1 x 32-bit register
- And a8...a15 fits in 1 x 32-bit register
- Total: 2 x 32-bit registers per thread for the full matrix A fragment

Each thread covers 16 columns of K (one 16-column group) across 8 rows (either rows 0-7 or rows 8-15), with 8 four-bit elements packed per 32-bit register.

==============================================================
6. ARROWS IN FIGURE
==============================================================
Two diagonal arrows are shown in the figure:
- First arrow: spans from approximately (row 1, col 32-47) diagonally to (row 7, col 0-15), indicating the continuation of the thread pattern (T8 through T27) filling rows 2-6 implicitly.
- Second arrow: spans from approximately (row 9, col 32-47) diagonally to (row 15, col 0-15), indicating the same continuation for the second row block (rows 10-14, threads T8-T27 with a8...a15).

  Row 13: Thread T_{2i+21},   all 32 bits [31..0]   (implied)
  Row 14: Thread T_{2i+25},   all 32 bits [31..0]   (implied)
  Row 15: Thread T_{2i+29},   all 32 bits [31..0]

Upper half uses even-indexed threads (T_{2i}, T_{2i+4}, T_{2i+8}, ..., T_{2i+28}).
Lower half uses odd-indexed threads (T_{2i+1}, T_{2i+5}, T_{2i+9}, ..., T_{2i+29}).
The step between consecutive rows within each half is +4 in thread index.

=============================================================
5. BIT-FIELD TO COLUMN GROUP MAPPING
=============================================================
Each thread holds a 32-bit metadata register. The 32 bits are divided into
eight 4-bit fields, each covering one column group of 8 columns:

  Bits [3..0]   -> columns 0..7
  Bits [7..4]   -> columns 8..15
  Bits [11..8]  -> columns 16..23
  Bits [15..12] -> columns 24..31
  Bits [19..16] -> columns 32..39
  Bits [23..20] -> columns 40..47
  Bits [27..24] -> columns 48..55
  Bits [31..28] -> columns 56..63

=============================================================
6. METADATA ENCODING FORMAT
=============================================================
For .u4/.s4 data type with k=64:
Each group of 8 columns contains 8 elements of 4-bit data.
With 2:4 sparsity, every 4 consecutive elements have 2 non-zeros.
A 4-bit metadata field encodes the positions of 2 non-zero elements
out of every 4 elements, using 2 bits per selected position (2 bits x 2 = 4 bits per group of 4 elements).

Within each 4-bit metadata field:
  Bits [1..0]: index (0,1,2,3) of the first non-zero element in the group of 4
  Bits [3..2]: index (0,1,2,3)

SPARSE MMA FRAGMENT LAYOUT DESCRIPTION
Operation: .m16n8k128
Matrix: B (input matrix)
Data types: .u4 / .s4 / .e2m1
Fragment identifier: %laneid:{fragments}

MATRIX DIMENSIONS AND CONTEXT
M = 16, N = 8, K = 128
This figure shows rows 0-31 of matrix B (the K dimension spans 128 elements; rows 0-31 correspond to the K-dimension rows of B)

GRID STRUCTURE
Rows (outer): Groups of matrix rows, labeled 0-7, 8-15, 16-23, 24-31
Columns (register index): 0, 1, 2, ... (ellipsis), 7
Total columns shown: 0, 1, 2, ..., 7 (8 register columns total)

THREAD-TO-ELEMENT MAPPING TABLE

Row group 0-7 (matrix rows 0 through 7):
  Column 0: T0  holds {b0, b1, .., b6, b7}
  Column 1: T4  holds {b0, b1, .., b6, b7}
  Column 2: (implied, continues pattern)
  ...
  Column 7: T28 holds {b0, b1, .., b6, b7}

Row group 8-15 (matrix rows 8 through 15):
  Column 0: T1  holds {b0, b1, .., b6, b7}
  Column 1: T5  holds {b0, b1, .., b6, b7}
  Column 2: (implied, continues pattern)
  ...
  Column 7: T29 holds {b0, b1, .., b6, b7}

Row group 16-23 (matrix rows 16 through 23):
  Column 0: T2  holds {b0, b1, .., b6, b7}
  Column 1: T6  holds {b0, b1, .., b6, b7}
  Column 2: (implied, continues pattern)
  ...
  Column 7: R30 holds {b0, b1, .., b6, b7}
  (Note: label shown as "R30" in figure, likely T30)

Row group 24-31 (matrix rows 24 through 31):
  Column 0: T3  holds {b0, b1, .., b6, b7}
  Column 1: T7  holds {b0, b1, .., b6, b7}
  Column 2: (implied, continues pattern)
  ...
  Column 7: T31 holds {b0, b1, .., b6, b7}

THREAD GROUPING PATTERN
Threads are assigned to row groups as follows:
  Threads T0, T4, T8, T12, T16, T20, T24, T28 cover matrix rows 0-7
  Threads T1, T5, T9, T13, T17, T21, T25, T29 cover matrix rows 8-15
  Threads T2, T6, T10, T14, T18, T22, T26, T30 cover matrix rows 16-23
  Threads T3, T7, T11, T15, T19, T23, T27, T31 cover matrix rows 24-31

Within each row group, the 8 threads (columns 0 through 7) correspond to threads spaced 4 apart:
  Column 0 = thread base (T0, T1, T2, T3)
  Column 1 = thread base + 4 (T4, T5, T6, T7)
  Column 2 = thread base + 8 (implied)
  ...
  Column 7 = thread base + 28 (T28, T29, T30, T31)

REGISTER ELEMENT PACKING
Each thread holds 8 elements per register column: b0, b1, b2 (shown as ..), b3, b4, b5 (shown as ..), b6, b7
The notation {b0, b1, .., b6, b7} indicates 8 packed elements (b0 through b7) stored in the register(s) for that thread at that column position.
Given the data types are 4-bit (.u4/.s4) or 2-bit (.e2m1), multiple elements are packed into each 32-bit register:
  For .u4/.s4: 8 x 4-bit = 32 bits = 1 register per column entry
  For .e2m1: 8 x 2-bit = 16 bits, potentially 2 column entries per register

ARROW IN FIGURE
A diagonal arrow spans from the upper-right area (row group 0-7, column 7 region) down to the lower area (row group 24-31, column 2 region), indicating the continuation of the pattern across the ellipsis columns (columns 2 through 6 are not explicitly shown but follow the same layout).

SPARSE MMA METADATA LAYOUT DESCRIPTION
========================================

1. MMA SHAPE AND DATA TYPES
----------------------------
Shape: .m16n8k128
Data types: .u4 (unsigned 4-bit), .s4 (signed 4-bit), .e2m1 (2-bit exponent, 1-bit mantissa)
This figure covers columns 0 through 63 (the first half of the k=128 dimension).

2. TABLE STRUCTURE
------------------
The table has rows 0–15 (16 rows total, corresponding to the m=16 dimension) and 8 column groups:
  Columns 0..7
  Columns 8..15
  Columns 16..23
  Columns 24..31
  Columns 32..39
  Columns 40..47
  Columns 48..55
  Columns 56..63

Rows 2–6 and rows 10–14 are omitted with "..." notation, indicating the same pattern continues.

3. THREAD ASSIGNMENTS AND BIT POSITIONS
-----------------------------------------
The notation is: T<thread_index>: [high_bit..low_bit]

The thread index uses a parameterized variable "i" where i ranges to cover all 32 threads in a warp.

ROW 0:
  Columns 0..7:    T_{2i}:     [3..0]
  Columns 8..15:   T_{2i}:     [7..4]
  Columns 16..23:  T_{2i}:     [11..8]
  Columns 24..31:  T_{2i}:     [15..12]
  Columns 32..39:  T_{2i}:     [19..16]
  Columns 40..47:  T_{2i}:     [23..20]
  Columns 48..55:  T_{2i}:     [27..24]
  Columns 56..63:  T_{2i}:     [31..28]

ROW 1:
  Columns 0..7:    T_{2i+4}:   [3..0]
  Columns 8..15:   T_{2i+4}:   [7..4]
  Columns 16..23:  T_{2i+4}:   [11..8]
  Columns 24..31:  T_{2i+4}:   [15..12]
  Columns 32..39:  T_{2i+4}:   [19..16]
  Columns 40..47:  T_{2i+4}:   [23..20]
  Columns 48..55:  T_{2i+4}:   [27..24]
  Columns 56..63:  T_{2i+4}:   [31..28]

ROW 7:
  Columns 0..7:    T_{2i+28}:  [3..0]
  Columns 8..15:   T_{2i+28}:  [7..4]
  Columns 16..23:  T_{2i+28}:  [11..8]
  Columns 24..31:  T_{2i+28}:  [15..12]
  Columns 32..39:  T_{2i+28}:  [19..16]
  Columns 40..47:  T_{2i+28}:  [23..20]
  Columns 48..55:  T_{2i+28}:  [27..24]
  Columns 56..63:  T_{2i+28}:  [31..28]

ROW 8:
  Columns 0..7:    T_{2i+1}:   [3..0]
  Columns 8..15:   T_{2i+1}:   [7..4]
  Columns 16..23:  T_{2i+1}:   [11..8]
  Columns 24..31:  T_{2i+1}:   [15..12]
  Columns 32..39:  T_{2i+1}:   [19..16]
  Columns 40..47:  T_{2i+1}:   [23..20]
  Columns 48..55:  T_{2i+1}:   [27..24]
  Columns 56..63:  T_{2i+1}:   [31..28]

ROW 9:
  Columns 0..7:    T_{2i+5}:   [3..0]
  Columns 8..15:   T_{2i+5}:   [7..4]
  Columns 16..23:  T_{2i+5}:   [11..8]
  Columns 24..31:  T_{2i+5}:   [15..12]
  Columns 32..39:  T_{2i+5}:   [19..16]
  Columns 40..47:  T_{2i+5}:   [23..20]
  Columns 48..55:  T_{2i+5}:   [27..24]
  Columns 56..63:  T_{2i+5}:   [31..28]

ROW 15:
  Columns 0..7:    T_{2i+29}:  [3..0]
  Columns 8..15:   T_{2i+29}:  [7..4]
  Columns 16..23:  T_{2i+29}:  [11..8]
  Columns 24..31:  T_{2i+29}:  [15..12]
  Columns 32..39:  T_{2i+29}:  [19..16]
  Columns 40..47:  T_{2i+29}:  [23..20]
  Columns 48..55:  T_{2i+29}:  [27..24]
  Columns 56..63:  T_{2i+29}:  [31..28]

4. METADATA ENCODING FORMAT
-----------------------------
Each thread holds a 32-bit metadata register. The 32 bits are divided into eight 4-bit fields:
  Bits [3..0]   -> metadata for columns 0..7
  Bits [7..4]   -> metadata for columns 8..15
  Bits [11..8]  -> metadata for columns 16..23
  Bits [15..12] -> metadata for columns 24..31
  Bits [19..16] -> metadata for columns 32..39
  Bits [23..20] -> metadata for columns 40..47
  Bits [27..24] -> metadata for columns 48..55
  Bits [31..28] -> metadata for columns 56..63

Each 4-bit field encodes the sparsity selection for 8 consecutive columns (one group of 8 elements). For 2:4 structured sparsity at 4-bit (u4/s4) or e2m1 precision, each group of 4 elements has 2 non-zero values selected by 2-bit indices, so 4 bits cover 2 selections of 2 bits each within the 8-column group.

5. THREAD GROUPING AND ROW PATTERNS
--------------------------------------
Rows 0–7 (upper half of m=16):
  Row 0 uses thread T_{2i}
  Row 1 uses thread T_{2i+4}
  Row 2 uses thread T_{2i+8}   (implied by pattern)
  Row 3 uses thread T_{2i+12}  (implied)
  Row 4 uses thread T_{2i+16}  (implied)
  Row 5 uses thread T_{2i+20}  (implied)
  Row 6 uses thread T_{2i+24}  (implied)
  Row 7 uses thread T_{2i+28}
  -> Thread offset increments by +4 per row for rows 0–7, using even-indexed base threads (T_{2i})

Rows 8–15 (lower half of m=16):
  Row 8  uses thread T_{2i+1}
  Row 9  uses thread T_{2i+5}
  Row 10 uses thread T_{2i+9}  (implied)
  Row 11 uses thread T_{2i+13} (implied)
  Row 12 uses thread T_{2i+17} (implied)
  Row 13 uses thread T_{2i+21} (implied)
  Row 14 uses thread T_{2i+25} (implied)
  Row 15 uses thread T_{2i+29}
  -> Thread offset increments by +4 per row for rows 8–15, using odd-indexed base threads (T_{2i+1})

SUMMARY OF KEY PATTERNS:
- Each thread holds exactly 32 bits of metadata covering all 8 column groups (0..63) for a single row.
- The 32-bit register is fully consumed by the 8 column groups (8 groups x 4 bits = 32 bits).
- Rows 0–7 are handled by even-offset threads (2i, 2i+4, ..., 2i+28).
- Rows 8–15 are handled by odd-offset threads (2i+1, 2i+5, ..., 2i+29).
- The parameter i indexes pairs of threads, stepping through the warp in groups of 2.
- This figure covers only columns 0–63; a companion figure covers columns 64–127 for the full k=128 extent.

Lower half uses odd-offset threads starting at T3 (mod 4 = 3)

==============================================================
5. BIT FIELD ASSIGNMENTS WITHIN EACH THREAD'S 32-BIT REGISTER
==============================================================
Each thread holds a 32-bit metadata register. The bits are distributed across the 8 column groups as follows:

Column group 64..71:   bits [3..0]   (4 bits)
Column group 72..79:   bits [7..4]   (4 bits)
Column group 80..87:   bits [11..8]  (4 bits)
Column group 88..95:   bits [15..12] (4 bits)
Column group 96..103:  bits [19..16] (4 bits)
Column group 104..111: bits [23..20] (4 bits)
Column group 112..119: bits [27..24] (4 bits)
Column group 120..127: bits [31..28] (4 bits)

Total: 8 groups x 4 bits = 32 bits per thread register

Each 4-bit field covers 8 columns. Within each 8-column span, 2-bit sub-fields encode which 2 of 4 elements are non-zero (2:4 structured sparsity). Two 2-bit selectors per 4-bit field = 2 pairs of elements selected from 4.

==============================================================
6. METADATA ENCODING FORMAT
==============================================================
For .u4/.s4/.e2m1 types with k=128 and 2:4 sparsity:
- Every group of 4 consecutive elements along the K dimension has exactly 2 non-zero values
- Each 2-bit selector encodes which 2 of 4 elements are retained
- 4 bits per 8-column group encodes two consecutive 2:4 groups (columns i..i+3 and i+4..i+7)
- The metadata register for each thread is 32 bits wide, covering all 8 column groups (64 columns total for this half)
- Combined with the companion figure (columns 0-63), the full 32-bit metadata register covers all k=128 columns

==============================================================
7. DIAGONAL ARROW ANNOTATIONS
==============================================================
Two diagonal arrows are visible in the figure:
Arrow 1: Points from the area of row 2 / column 88..95 leftward-downward toward row 7 / column 72..79, indicating the continuation of the thread pattern (T10 through T30) fills rows 2-7.
Arrow 2: Points from the area of row 10 / column 104..111 rightward-upward toward row 10 / column 120..127, indicating the continuation of the thread pattern (T11 through T31) fills rows 10-15.
These arrows visually indicate that the blank rows follow the same thread-increment-by-4 pattern as the explicitly shown rows.

==============================================================
8. RELATIONSHIP TO FULL k=128 LAYOUT
==============================================================
This figure covers columns 64-127 (the second 64 columns of k=128).

async-warpgroup-smem-layout-128B-mn.png:
MN Major 128B Swizzling - Complete Description

CONCEPT OVERVIEW
This figure illustrates the 128-byte swizzling pattern for MN-major layout in NVIDIA GPU shared memory. Swizzling is a technique used to avoid bank conflicts when accessing shared memory. The figure shows how 64 elements (numbered 0 through 63) are mapped to an 8x8 grid of memory locations using a swizzled addressing scheme.

AXES AND DIMENSIONS
- Horizontal axis (left to right): K dimension, labeled "K dim", with column indices 0 through 7
- Vertical axis (top to bottom): M/N dimension, labeled "M/N dim (contiguous)", with row indices 0 through 7
- The M/N dimension is explicitly noted as the contiguous dimension

GRID STRUCTURE
The grid is 8 rows by 8 columns, totaling 64 cells. Each cell contains a unique integer from 0 to 63.

COLOR CODING
Four colors are used to indicate swizzle groupings or bank assignments:
- Purple/Blue (lavender)
- Green (light green)
- Yellow (light yellow)
- Pink/Red (light red/salmon)

COMPLETE CELL VALUES (row, column -> value and color):

Row 0: col0=0(purple), col1=9(green), col2=18(yellow), col3=27(red), col4=36(green), col5=45(purple), col6=54(yellow), col7=63(red)

Row 1: col0=1(green), col1=8(purple), col2=19(red), col3=26(yellow), col4=37(yellow), col5=44(red), col6=55(purple), col7=62(green)

Row 2: col0=2(yellow), col1=11(red), col2=16(purple), col3=25(green), col4=38(purple), col5=47(green), col6=52(red), col7=61(yellow)

Row 3: col0=3(red), col1=10(yellow), col2=17(green), col3=24(purple), col4=39(red), col5=46(yellow), col6=53(green), col7=60(purple)

Row 4: col0=4(purple), col1=13(green), col2=22(yellow), col3=31(red), col4=32(green), col5=41(purple), col6=50(yellow), col7=59(red)

Row 5: col0=5(green), col1=12(purple), col2=23(red), col3=30(yellow), col4=33(yellow), col5=40(red), col6=51(purple), col7=58(green)

Row 6: col0=6(yellow), col1=15(red), col2=20(purple), col3=29(green), col4=34(purple), col5=43(green), col6=48(red), col7=57(yellow)

Row 7: col0=7(red), col1=14(yellow), col2=21(green), col3=28(purple), col4=35(red), col5=42(yellow), col6=49(green), col7=56(purple)

NUMBERING PATTERN ANALYSIS
The element numbers do not follow a simple row-major or column-major sequential order. Instead:
- Reading down column 0: 0,1,2,3,4,5,6,7 (sequential, stride 1)
- Reading down column 1: 9,8,11,10,13,12,15,14 (pairs swapped)
- Reading down column 2: 18,19,16,17,22,23,20,21 (pairs swapped with offset)
- Reading down column 3: 27,26,25,24,31,30,29,28 (descending groups of 4)
- Reading down column 4: 36,37,38,39,32,33,34,35 (groups of 4 swapped)
- Reading down column 5: 45,44,47,46,41,40,43,42
- Reading down column 6: 54,55,52,53,50,51,48,49
- Reading down column 7: 63,62,61,60,59,58,57,56 (descending sequential)

The M/N dimension (rows) is contiguous, meaning elements 0-7 are stored contiguously in memory along column 0. The swizzle pattern XORs the column index with a function of the row index to redistribute elements across memory banks, preventing bank conflicts during K-dimension access patterns.

COLOR PATTERN
The color of each cell follows a 4-color pattern that rotates based on a XOR of row and column indices, consistent with a 128-byte swizzle period across the 8-column (K) dimension. Each color represents one of four 32-byte (or similar) bank groups, ensuring that simultaneous accesses along the K dimension hit different banks.
async-warpgroup-smem-layout-128B-k.png:
K MAJOR 128B SWIZZLING - COMPLETE FIGURE DESCRIPTION

CONCEPT OVERVIEW:
This figure illustrates the memory layout pattern for "K major 128B swizzling" as used in NVIDIA GPU tensor/matrix operations. It shows how logical matrix elements are mapped to physical memory locations (or vice versa) when the K dimension is the contiguous (major) dimension in memory, with 128-byte swizzling applied to avoid bank conflicts in shared memory.

AXES AND DIMENSIONS:
- Horizontal axis: labeled "K dim (contiguous)" with an arrow pointing right. Columns are indexed 0 through 7.
- Vertical axis: labeled "M/N dim" with an arrow pointing downward. Rows are indexed 0 through 7.
- The matrix is 8 rows x 8 columns, for a total of 64 elements (numbered 0 through 63).

FULL GRID OF VALUES (row, column -> value):
Row 0: col0=0,  col1=1,  col2=2,  col3=3,  col4=4,  col5=5,  col6=6,  col7=7
Row 1: col0=9,  col1=8,  col2=11, col3=10, col4=13, col5=12, col6=15, col7=14
Row 2: col0=18, col1=19, col2=16, col3=17, col4=22, col5=23, col6=20, col7=21
Row 3: col0=27, col1=26, col2=25, col3=24, col4=31, col5=30, col6=29, col7=28
Row 4: col0=36, col1=37, col2=38, col3=39, col4=32, col5=33, col6=34, col7=35
Row 5: col0=45, col1=44, col2=47, col3=46, col4=41, col5=40, col6=43, col7=42
Row 6: col0=54, col1=55, col2=52, col3=53, col4=50, col5=51, col6=48, col7=49
Row 7: col0=63, col1=62, col2=61, col3=60, col4=59, col5=58, col6=57, col7=56

COLOR CODING (4 distinct colors used to indicate swizzle groups/phases):
The cells are colored with four colors: blue/purple, green, yellow/tan, and red/pink. The color pattern encodes the swizzle phase for each cell. The color assignment per cell is as follows:

Row 0: blue(0), green(1), yellow(2), red(3), blue(4), green(5), yellow(6), red(7)
Row 1: green(9), blue(8), red(11), yellow(10), green(13), blue(12), red(15), yellow(14)
Row 2: yellow(18), red(19), blue(16), green(17), yellow(22), red(23), blue(20), green(21)
Row 3: red(27), yellow(26), green(25), blue(24), red(31), yellow(30), green(29), blue(28)
Row 4: blue(36), green(37), yellow(38), red(39), blue(32), green(33), yellow(34), red(35)
Row 5: green(45), blue(44), red(47), yellow(46), green(41), blue(40), red(43), yellow(42)
Row 6: yellow(54), red(55), blue(52), green(53), yellow(50), red(51), blue(48), green(49)
Row 7: red(63), yellow(62), green(61), blue(60), red(59), yellow(58), green(57), blue(56)

COLOR PATTERN ANALYSIS:
The 4-color pattern rotates both across columns and across rows, forming a 4x4 repeating XOR-based swizzle pattern. Each row shifts the color phase by one position relative to the previous row. This is characteristic of XOR-based swizzling where the swizzle phase = (row XOR col) mod 4.

NUMERICAL PATTERN ANALYSIS:
The numbers represent physical memory addresses (or tile indices) assigned to logical positions. Key observations:
- Row 0 is sequential: 0,1,2,3,4,5,6,7 (no swizzle offset in first row)
- Each subsequent row applies an increasing XOR-based permutation within groups of elements
- The values increase by 9 per row in the first column (0,9,18,27,36,45,54,63), suggesting a stride of 9 elements per M/N step
- Within each row, pairs of adjacent elements are swapped or reordered according to the swizzle pattern
- The right half of the grid (columns 4-7) contains values offset from the left half (columns 0-3) by 4 in row 0, but the offset changes with swizzling in subsequent rows

SWIZZLE PATTERN DETAIL:
The 128B swizzling with K-major layout means that within each 128-byte aligned block, the column (K dimension) index is XORed with a function of the row index to determine the actual memory column accessed. This prevents multiple rows from mapping to the same shared memory bank when accessed simultaneously by a warp.

The pattern in the left 4 columns (0-3) and right 4 columns (4-7) appears to be independently swizzled, suggesting the 128B boundary falls between column 3 and column 4 (i.e., each half-row of 4 elements occupies 64 bytes if elements are 16 bytes each, or the full 8 columns = 128 bytes for smaller element sizes).

LAYOUT CONTEXT:
This figure is part of NVIDIA PTX ISA documentation describing shared memory swizzling for matrix operations (likely wgmma or ldmatrix instructions). The swizzling ensures conflict-free shared memory access patterns when loading matrix tiles for tensor core operations.

CONCEPT OVERVIEW
This figure illustrates the memory layout pattern for "MN major 64B swizzling" as used in NVIDIA PTX ISA matrix operations. It shows how matrix elements are mapped to memory locations (indicated by sequential numbers 0-31) when using a swizzled layout with 64-byte swizzle granularity. The swizzling reorders elements to avoid bank conflicts in shared memory.

AXES AND DIMENSIONS
- Horizontal axis (top): K dim, labeled with column indices 0 through 7 (8 columns total)
- Vertical axis (left): M/N dim (contiguous), labeled with row indices 0 through 3 (4 rows total)

ELEMENT LAYOUT TABLE
The numbers inside each cell represent the sequential memory address/offset order:

         K=0  K=1  K=2  K=3  K=4  K=5  K=6  K=7
Row 0:    0    4    9   13   18   22   27   31
Row 1:    1    5    8   12   19   23   26   30

Row 3:    3    7   10   14   17   21   24   28

COLOR CODING
Each cell is color-coded to indicate which 64-byte swizzle group or bank it belongs to. Four distinct colors are used:

Purple/Blue cells: (0,0)=0, (1,2)=8, (2,4)=16, (3,6)=24
Green cells: (0,1)=4, (1,0)=1, (0,2)=9, (1,1)=5, (2,5)=20, (3,4)=17, (2,6)=25, (3,7)=28 and others
Yellow/Light cells: (0,3)=13, (1,3)=12, (2,2)=11, (3,2)=10, (0,4)=18, (2,3)=15, (3,3)=14, (2,7)=29, (3,5)=21 and others

Precise color groupings by position (row, col):

Green: (1,0), (0,1), (0,2), (1,1), (3,4), (2,5), (2,6), (3,7)

KEY OBSERVATIONS
1. Within each column of the M/N (contiguous) dimension, elements are stored consecutively: column K=0 contains addresses 0,1,2,3 (sequential), showing the M/N dimension is contiguous in memory.
2. The swizzling pattern shifts the starting offset for each row as K increases, creating a diagonal pattern of color groups across the matrix.
3. The sequential numbering (0-31) represents the physical memory order, and the swizzle pattern ensures that elements accessed simultaneously by different threads map to different memory banks, avoiding conflicts.
4. The 64B swizzle period corresponds to groups of elements that fit within a 64-byte aligned block, with the swizzle XOR pattern rotating which physical bank each logical position maps to per row.

CONCEPT OVERVIEW:
This figure illustrates the memory layout pattern for "K major 64B swizzling" as used in NVIDIA GPU tensor/matrix operations. It shows how logical matrix elements are mapped to physical memory locations (represented by the numbers inside each cell) when the K dimension is the contiguous (major) dimension and 64-byte swizzling is applied.

AXES AND DIMENSIONS:
- Horizontal axis: K dim (contiguous) — labeled at the top right with a rightward arrow. Column indices are 0, 1, 2, 3 (left to right).
- Vertical axis: M/N dim — labeled on the left side with a downward arrow. Row indices are 0, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7 (top to bottom).

GRID STRUCTURE:
The matrix is 8 rows by 4 columns, giving 32 total cells. Each cell contains a number representing the physical memory slot (0 through 31) assigned to that logical position.

CELL VALUES (row, column -> value):
Row 0: col0=0,  col1=1,  col2=2,  col3=3
Row 1: col0=4,  col1=5,  col2=6,  col3=7
Row 2: col0=9,  col1=8,  col2=11, col3=10
Row 3: col0=13, col1=12, col2=15, col3=14
Row 4: col0=18, col1=19, col2=16, col3=17
Row 5: col0=22, col1=23, col2=20, col3=21
Row 6: col0=27, col1=26, col2=25, col3=24
Row 7: col0=31, col1=30, col2=29, col3=28

COLOR CODING (each color represents a distinct swizzle group/bank):
The cells are colored with four distinct colors — purple/blue, green, yellow/lime, and red/pink — indicating which swizzle group each element belongs to. The color pattern shifts across rows, implementing the swizzle pattern. Detailed color assignments per cell:

Row 0: col0=purple, col1=green, col2=yellow, col3=red
Row 1: col0=purple, col1=green, col2=yellow(implied light), col3=purple(light)
Row 2: col0=green,  col1=purple, col2=red,   col3=yellow
Row 3: col0=green,  col1=purple, col2=green(light), col3=yellow(light)
Row 4: col0=yellow, col1=red,    col2=purple, col3=green
Row 5: col0=yellow, col1=red,    col2=purple(light), col3=green(light)
Row 6: col0=red,    col1=yellow, col2=green,  col3=purple
Row 7: col0=red,    col1=yellow, col2=green,  col3=purple

SWIZZLE PATTERN OBSERVATIONS:
- Rows 0-1 share the same color pattern: purple, green, yellow, red
- Rows 2-3 shift the pattern: green, purple, red, yellow
- Rows 4-5 shift again: yellow, red, purple, green
- Rows 6-7 shift again: red, yellow, green, purple
- Each pair of rows (0-1, 2-3, 4-5, 6-7) uses the same color assignment, and the pattern rotates by one position every two rows
- The physical memory indices within each color group are not sequential in the logical layout, demonstrating the swizzle reordering
- The numbering within rows is not always sequential (e.g., row 2 has 9,8,11,10 instead of 8,9,10,11), confirming that swizzling reorders elements within a row as well

PURPOSE:
This swizzling scheme is used to avoid shared memory bank conflicts when accessing matrix data in K-major order on NVIDIA GPUs. The 64B swizzle pattern ensures that threads accessing different rows of the same column land in different memory banks.

CONCEPT OVERVIEW:
This figure illustrates the memory layout and swizzling pattern for MN major 32-byte (32B) swizzling in NVIDIA GPU tensor memory. It shows how data elements are arranged and colored to indicate which 32-byte swizzle group they belong to, across a 2-row by 8-column logical grid.

AXES AND DIMENSIONS:
- Horizontal axis (left to right): K dimension, labeled "K dim" with a rightward arrow at the top. Columns are indexed 0 through 7.
- Vertical axis (top to bottom): M/N dimension, labeled "M/N dim (contiguous)" with a downward arrow on the left side. Rows are indexed 0 and 1.

GRID STRUCTURE:
The grid is 2 rows by 8 columns, for a total of 16 cells. Each cell contains a sequential integer value.

CELL VALUES BY POSITION (row, column) -> value:
Row 0: (0,0)=0, (0,1)=2, (0,2)=4, (0,3)=6, (0,4)=9, (0,5)=11, (0,6)=13, (0,7)=15
Row 1: (1,0)=1, (1,1)=3, (1,2)=5, (1,3)=7, (1,4)=8, (1,5)=10, (1,6)=12, (1,7)=14

COLOR CODING (indicating swizzle groups):
The cells are colored with four distinct colors: purple/blue, yellow/green-yellow, green, and red/pink. The color pattern indicates which 32-byte swizzle bank each element maps to.

Color assignments by cell:
Row 0:
- (0,0)=0: purple/blue
- (0,1)=2: yellow
- (0,2)=4: purple/blue
- (0,3)=6: yellow
- (0,4)=9: green
- (0,5)=11: red/pink
- (0,6)=13: green
- (0,7)=15: red/pink

Row 1:
- (1,0)=1: green
- (1,1)=3: red/pink
- (1,2)=5: green
- (1,3)=7: red/pink
- (1,4)=8: purple/blue
- (1,5)=10: yellow
- (1,6)=12: purple/blue
- (1,7)=14: yellow

DATA ORGANIZATION PATTERN:
- The numbering is interleaved: column 0 contains values 0 and 1 (row 0 and row 1 respectively), column 1 contains 2 and 3, column 2 contains 4 and 5, and so on. This reflects that the M/N dimension is contiguous in memory, so consecutive integers alternate between row 0 and row 1.
- The swizzle pattern (color grouping) shifts between the two rows, demonstrating the XOR-based swizzling that maps different (row, column) combinations to different 32-byte aligned memory banks to avoid bank conflicts.
- The color pattern in row 1 is offset relative to row 0, specifically the color groups appear shifted by 4 columns, which is characteristic of a 32B swizzle pattern applied to an MN-major layout.

SUMMARY:
The figure shows a 2xK (K=8) logical matrix where the M/N dimension is the contiguous (fast-varying) dimension. The 16 elements are numbered 0-15 in M/N-major order (column-major within this view). The four colors represent four distinct 32-byte swizzle groups, and the swizzle pattern is XOR-shifted between rows to distribute accesses across different memory banks, reducing shared memory bank conflicts during tensor operations.
async-warpgroup-smem-layout-32B-k.png:
K Major 32B Swizzling - Complete Description

CONCEPT OVERVIEW
This figure illustrates a K-major 32-byte swizzling pattern used in NVIDIA GPU shared memory layout for matrix operations. Swizzling is a technique that remaps logical tile indices to physical memory locations to avoid shared memory bank conflicts. This specific pattern is for K-major (K dimension is contiguous in memory) data layout.

AXIS LABELS AND ORIENTATION
- Horizontal axis (top): "K dim (contiguous)" with a right-pointing arrow. Column indices are 0 and 1.
- Vertical axis (left): "M/N dim" with a downward-pointing arrow. Row indices are 0 through 7.

GRID STRUCTURE
The grid is 8 rows by 2 columns, for a total of 16 cells. Each cell contains a single integer value representing the physical memory tile index (or swizzled address offset).

CELL VALUES (row, column -> value)
Row 0: col 0 = 0,  col 1 = 1
Row 1: col 0 = 2,  col 1 = 3
Row 2: col 0 = 4,  col 1 = 5
Row 3: col 0 = 6,  col 1 = 7
Row 4: col 0 = 9,  col 1 = 8
Row 5: col 0 = 11, col 1 = 10
Row 6: col 0 = 13, col 1 = 12
Row 7: col 0 = 15, col 1 = 14

COLOR CODING OF CELLS
Colors indicate groupings or bank assignments. The color pattern is:

Row 0: col 0 = blue/purple,   col 1 = green
Row 1: col 0 = yellow/tan,    col 1 = pink/salmon (red)
Row 2: col 0 = blue/purple,   col 1 = green
Row 3: col 0 = yellow/tan,    col 1 = pink/salmon
Row 4: col 0 = green,         col 1 = blue/purple
Row 5: col 0 = pink/salmon,   col 1 = yellow/tan
Row 6: col 0 = green,         col 1 = blue/purple
Row 7: col 0 = pink/salmon,   col 1 = yellow/tan

SWIZZLING PATTERN ANALYSIS
- Rows 0-3: The values are sequential (0,1,2,3,4,5,6,7) and the column order is NOT swapped (col 0 < col 1 in each row).
- Rows 4-7: The values are still sequential overall (8,9,10,11,12,13,14,15) but the column order IS swapped within each row (col 0 > col 1 in each row: 9>8, 11>10, 13>12, 15>14).
- This XOR-based swizzle pattern means that in the second half of the M/N dimension (rows 4-7), the K-dimension columns are swapped relative to the first half (rows 0-3), which is characteristic of a 32-byte swizzle applied to avoid bank conflicts.
- The color pattern also shifts/rotates between the top half and bottom half, reflecting the swizzled remapping.

KEY TAKEAWAY FOR PROGRAMMERS
In K-major 32B swizzling, the physical tile index assigned to logical position (m_or_n_row, k_col) is swizzled such that for rows 4-7, the K column index is XORed with 1 (columns 0 and 1 are swapped). This ensures that threads accessing different M/N rows but the same K column land on different shared memory banks, improving memory access efficiency.

- Rows 1, 5, 9, 13: Green background
- Rows 2, 6, 10, 14: Yellow/light yellow background
- Rows 3, 7, 11, 15: Pink/salmon/red background

This 4-row color grouping repeats four times across the 16 rows, likely indicating warp-level or thread-group-level data partitioning.

AXIS LABELS:
- Top axis (columns): labeled 0 through 15 from left to right
- Left axis (rows): labeled 0 through 15 from top to bottom
- No additional axis titles are shown in the figure itself; the caption provides the context: K major, no-swizzling, tf32 type

SUMMARY OF DATA ORGANIZATION:
The layout divides the 16x16 tile into four 16x4 vertical slabs. Each slab holds 64 consecutive elements. Within each slab, elements are stored in row-major order with 4 elements per row. The K-major designation means the K (inner/reduction) dimension varies fastest within each group of 4 consecutive elements. The no-swizzling designation means there is no XOR-based address permutation applied to avoid bank conflicts. The tf32 type means each element is a 32-bit tensor float value, so each element occupies 4 bytes in memory.

SWIZZLE PATTERN ANALYSIS:
The tf32 type is 4 bytes per element. With 32-byte swizzling, groups of 8 tf32 elements (8 x 4 = 32 bytes) form a swizzle unit.

The column axis (0-15) represents the K dimension (K-major layout). The row axis (0-15) represents the other matrix dimension.

Key observations about the swizzle:
- Rows 0-3 (first group of 4): columns 0-7 hold indices 0-31 sequentially (no swap), columns 8-15 hold indices 128-159 sequentially.
- Rows 4-7 (second group of 4): columns 0-3 and 4-7 are swapped relative to sequential order (e.g., row 4 has 36,37,38,39 in cols 0-3 and 32,33,34,35 in cols 4-7), and similarly for the right half.
- Rows 8-11 (third group of 4): same pattern as rows 0-3 (no swap within the half-row groups).
- Rows 12-15 (fourth group of 4): same swap pattern as rows 4-7.

The swizzle effectively XORs the column group index with a function of the row index to avoid shared memory bank conflicts when loading tf32 data in K-major order. The left half (columns 0-7) contains indices in the range 0-127 and the right half (columns 8-15) contains indices in the range 128-255, with the swizzle applied within each half independently.

- Row group: rows 0-7 are in the top half, rows 8-15 are in the bottom half.
- Within each 8x8 block, the index = block_base + (col_within_block * 8) + row_within_block.
- Block bases: top-left=0, bottom-left=64, top-right=128, bottom-right=192.

tensor-memory-layout.png:
TENSOR MEMORY LAYOUT AND ADDRESSING

OVERVIEW:
This figure shows a 2D tensor memory layout organized as a grid of lanes (rows) and columns, with explicit address values for each element position. The layout is row-major, where each lane (row) contains a contiguous 2KB block of memory spanning 512 columns.

DIMENSIONS:
- Number of lanes (rows): 128 (Lane 0 through Lane 127)
- Number of columns per lane: 512 (Col 0 through Col 511)
- Size per lane: 2KB (2 kilobytes)
- Total memory: 128 lanes x 2KB = 256KB

VISUAL STRUCTURE:
Each lane is represented as a wide horizontal orange/yellow rectangle labeled "2KB" in the center. The lanes are stacked vertically from Lane 0 at the top to Lane 127 at the bottom. Between Lane 2 and Lane 127, an ellipsis (...) indicates the intermediate lanes are not shown.

COLUMN LABELS (top of figure):
Col 0, Col 1, Col 2, ..., Col 511

ADDRESS FORMAT:
Addresses are expressed in the format 0xLLLL.CCCC where:
- LLLL = lane number in hexadecimal (upper 16 bits)
- CCCC = column number in hexadecimal (lower 16 bits)

ADDRESS VALUES BY LANE:

Lane 0 (lane index 0x0000):
- Col 0: 0x0000.0000
- Col 1: 0x0000.0001
- Col 2: 0x0000.0002
- ...
- Col 511: 0x0000.01FF

Lane 1 (lane index 0x0001):
- Col 0: 0x0001.0000
- Col 1: 0x0001.0001
- Col 2: 0x0001.0002
- ...
- Col 511: 0x0001.01FF

Lane 2 (lane index 0x0002):
- Col 0: 0x0002.0000
- Col 1: 0x0002.0001
- Col 2: 0x0002.0002
- ...
- Col 511: 0x0002.01FF

Lane 127 (lane index 0x007F):
- Col 0: 0x007F.0000
- Col 1: 0x007F.0001
- Col 2: 0x007F.0002
- ...
- Col 511: 0x007F.01FF

ADDRESS CALCULATION PATTERN:
Address = (lane_number << 16) | column_number
- Lane number occupies bits [31:16] (upper 16 bits)
- Column number occupies bits [15:0] (lower 16 bits)
- Column 511 = 0x01FF confirms 9-bit column addressing (512 columns = 2^9)
- Each lane stride = 0x00010000 (65536 in decimal)
- Each column stride within a lane = 0x00000001 (1 unit)

DIRECTIONAL ARROWS:
- A horizontal blue arrow points left-to-right along the column dimension (between Lane 0 and Lane 1 rows), indicating increasing column addresses from Col 0 to Col 511
- A vertical blue arrow points top-to-bottom along the lane dimension (at the Col 0 position), indicating increasing lane addresses from Lane 0 to Lane 127

DATA ORGANIZATION:
- Row-major layout: columns within a lane are contiguous in address space
- Each lane is a separate 2KB row with addresses starting at 0xLLLL.0000 and ending at 0xLLLL.01FF
- Lanes are separated by a fixed stride of 0x00010000 in the address space
- The addressing scheme encodes both the lane (row) and column index directly into the address word

tcgen05-ld-st-pack-unpack.png:
OVERVIEW OF THE FIGURE

This figure illustrates the Pack and Unpack operations for the tcgen05 load (ld) and store (st) instructions in NVIDIA PTX ISA. Specifically, it shows how 16-bit data elements are packed or unpacked when moving between a wide (packed) register layout and a narrow (unpacked) register layout across 32 threads (T0 through T31), each holding register values r0 and r1.

There are two named operations shown:
- tcgen05.st.unpack::16b — shown by arrows going from the LEFT (wide/packed) layout to the RIGHT (narrow/unpacked) layouts. Direction: left-to-right (top arrows).
- tcgen05.ld.pack::16b — shown by arrows going from the RIGHT (narrow/unpacked) layouts back to the LEFT (wide/packed) layout. Direction: right-to-left (bottom arrows).

---

LAYOUT DESCRIPTIONS

LEFT SIDE — PACKED LAYOUT (Wide, 32-bit per thread register)

This is a single tall column grid with 4 columns and 16 rows (covering threads T0–T31, registers r0 and r1).

The grid is 4 columns wide, labeled with a "32-bits" bracket spanning all 4 columns at the top.

Each cell contains a label of the form "Tx : ry" where x is the thread index (0–31) and y is the register index (0 or 1).

The 4 columns represent 4 separate 32-bit register slots (or sub-columns), each 8 bits wide (4 x 8 = 32 bits total per row).

Row-by-row contents (top to bottom), columns left to right:
Row 1:  T0:r0  | T1:r0  | T2:r0  | T3:r0
Row 2:  T4:r0  | T5:r0  | T6:r0  | T7:r0
Row 3:  T8:r0  | T9:r0  | T10:r0 | T11:r0
Row 4:  T12:r0 | T13:r0 | T14:r0 | T15:r0
Row 5:  T16:r0 | T17:r0 | T18:r0 | T19:r0
Row 6:  T20:r0 | T21:r0 | T22:r0 | T23:r0
Row 7:  T24:r0 | T25:r0 | T26:r0 | T27:r0
Row 8:  T28:r0 | T29:r0 | T30:r0 | T31:r0
Row 9:  T0:r1  | T1:r1  | T2:r1  | T3:r1
Row 10: T4:r1  | T5:r1  | T6:r1  | T7:r1
Row 11: T8:r1  | T9:r1  | T10:r1 | T11:r1
Row 12: T12:r1 | T13:r1 | T14:r1 | T15:r1
Row 13: T16:r1 | T17:r1 | T18:r1 | T19:r1
Row 14: T20:r1 | T21:r1 | T22:r1 | T23:r1
Row 15: T24:r1 | T25:r1 | T26:r1 | T27:r1
Row 16: T28:r1 | T29:r1 | T30:r1 | T31:r1

Color coding in the packed layout: Each thread's data is color-coded. The colors cycle through a set (green, yellow-green, teal/cyan, orange, gold, dark gray, blue, red-orange, etc.) to visually distinguish which thread's data maps to which unpacked column on the right side.

---

RIGHT SIDE — UNPACKED LAYOUT (Narrow, 16-bit per thread register)

There are 8 narrow single-column grids on the right side, arranged in pairs. Each narrow column contains 16 rows (T0–T31 for r0 and r1, but only half the threads per column compared to the packed layout).

Each narrow column is labeled "16-bits" at the bottom (two of the leftmost narrow columns are explicitly labeled "16-bits" each, with a bracket showing that two 16-bit columns together form "32-bits", and two 32-bit pairs form "64-bits").

The 8 narrow columns are grouped as follows (left to right on the right side):

GROUP 1 (leftmost pair, under "32-bits" bracket, under "64-bits" bracket):
Column A (16-bits):
T0:r0, T4:r0, T8:r0, T12:r0, T16:r0, T20:r0, T24:r0, T28:r0,
T0:r1, T4:r1, T8:r1, T12:r1, T16:r1, T20:r1, T24:r1, T28:r1

Column B (16-bits):
T0:r0, T4:r0, T8:r0, T12:r0, T16:r0, T20:r0, T24:r0, T28:r0,
T0:r1, T4:r1, T8:r1, T12:r1, T16:r1, T20:r1, T24:r1, T28:r1

(Columns A and B together = 32-bits; these correspond to threads T0, T4, T8, T12, T16, T20, T24, T28 — i.e., every 4th thread starting from T0)

GROUP 2 (second pair, also under "64-bits" bracket):
Column C (16-bits): T1:r0, T5:r0, T9:r0, T13:r0, T17:r0, T21:r0, T25:r0, T29:r0, T1:r1, T5:r1, T9:r1, T13:r1, T17:r1, T21:r1, T25:r1, T29:r1
Column D (16-bits): T1:r0, T5:r0, T9:r0, T13:r0, T17:r0, T21:r0, T25:r0, T29:r0, T1:r1, T5:r1, T9:r1, T13:r1, T17:r1, T21:r1, T25:r1, T29:r1

(Threads T1, T5, T9, T13, T17, T21, T25, T29)

GROUP 3 (third pair):
Column E: T2:r0, T6:r0, T10:r0, T14:r0, T18:r0, T22:r0, T26:r0, T30:r0, T2:r1, T6:r1, T10:r1, T14:r1, T18:r1, T22:r1, T26:r1, T30:r1
Column F: T2:r0, T6:r0, T10:r0, T14:r0, T18:r0, T22:r0, T26:r0, T30:r0, T2:r1, T6:r1, T10:r1, T14:r1, T18:r1, T22:r1, T26:r1, T30:r1

(Threads T2, T6, T10, T14, T18, T22, T26, T30)

GROUP 4 (rightmost pair):
Column G: T3:r0, T7:r0, T11:r0, T15:r0, T19:r0, T23:r0, T27:r0, T31:r0, T3:r1, T7:r1, T11:r1, T15:r1, T19:r1, T23:r1, T27:r1, T31:r1
Column H: T3:r0, T7:r0, T11:r0, T15:r0, T19:r0, T23:r0, T27:r0, T31:r0, T3:r1, T7:r1, T11:r1, T15:r1, T19:r1, T23:r1, T27:r1, T31:r1

(Threads T3, T7, T11, T15, T19, T23, T27, T31)

---

BIT-WIDTH ANNOTATIONS

- Each narrow (unpacked) column = 16-bits (labeled at the bottom of the two leftmost narrow columns)
- Two narrow columns together = 32-bits (bracket labeled "32-bits" spanning columns A+B)
- Four narrow columns together (two pairs) = 64-bits (bracket labeled "64-bits" spanning columns A+B+C+D)
- The packed left layout has a "32-bits" bracket spanning all 4 sub-columns

---

ARROWS AND DATA FLOW

TOP ARROWS (tcgen05.st.unpack::16b):
- A wide blue arrow at the top goes from the packed left layout rightward to the 8 unpacked narrow columns on the right.
- The label "tcgen05.st.unpack::16b" is written above this arrow.
- This indicates: when storing with unpack::16b, the 32-bit packed register data from each thread is split (unpacked) into two 16-bit values, distributed across two separate narrow columns.

BOTTOM ARROWS (tcgen05.ld.pack::16b):
- Blue arrows at the bottom go from the narrow unpacked columns (specifically the two leftmost narrow columns, columns A and B) back to the packed left layout.
- The label "tcgen05.ld.pack::16b" is written below these arrows.
- This indicates: when loading with pack::16b, two 16-bit values from two separate narrow columns are packed
tcgen05-decompression-4b8b.png:
CONCEPT BEING ILLUSTRATED:
This figure illustrates the decompression operation that occurs when copying data from Shared Memory to Tensor Memory using the tcgen05.cp instruction. Specifically, it shows how 4-bit packed data (.b4x16_p64 format) stored in Shared Memory is decompressed/expanded into 8-bit data (.b8x16 format) in Tensor Memory. Each 4-bit element is unpacked into an 8-bit element, effectively doubling the number of bytes used to represent the same data.

COMPONENTS AND LAYOUT:

Top Section - Shared Memory:
- Label: "Shared Memory" appears at the top left
- Format label: ".b4x16_p64" appears above the right-center portion of the memory bar
- Represented as a single horizontal bar containing many small cells (columns)
- The bar is narrower/shorter in total visual width compared to the Tensor Memory bar below, reflecting the packed/compressed nature of the 4-bit data
- The cells are colored in a repeating pattern of colors: green, white/light, blue/light blue, yellow/orange, peach/salmon, green, white, yellow, and other pastel shades
- The colored region occupies roughly the left half to two-thirds of the bar, with the right portion appearing mostly white/empty (uncolored cells)
- Black vertical dividers separate individual cells
- The colored cells represent the actual 4-bit packed data elements

Arrow (Data Flow):
- A solid blue downward-pointing arrow is positioned in the center between the two memory bars
- Label: "tcgen05.cp" appears to the right of the arrow
- This indicates the PTX instruction that performs the copy and decompression operation

Bottom Section - Tensor Memory:
- Label: "Tensor Memory" appears at the bottom left
- Format label: ".b8x16" appears below the center of the memory bar
- Represented as a single horizontal bar that is visually wider/longer than the Shared Memory bar above
- The bar contains more cells than the Shared Memory bar, and ALL cells across the entire bar are colored (no white/empty cells visible)
- The same color pattern is present but now spread across the full width of the bar: green, blue/light blue, yellow/orange, peach/salmon, green, blue, yellow, peach, and repeating pastel colors
- The colors correspond to the same data elements as in Shared Memory but each element now occupies a wider (8-bit) cell
- Black vertical dividers separate individual cells

NUMERICAL VALUES AND DIMENSIONS:
- Source format: .b4x16_p64 — 4-bit elements, 16 elements per group, packed with 64-bit packing
- Destination format: .b8x16 — 8-bit elements, 16 elements per group
- The decompression ratio is 2:1 in terms of bits per element (4-bit to 8-bit), meaning the data expands to twice the storage size
- The Tensor Memory bar visually appears approximately twice as wide as the populated (colored) portion of the Shared Memory bar, consistent with the 2x expansion

DATA FLOW AND ORGANIZATION PATTERN:
- Data flows from top (Shared Memory) to bottom (Tensor Memory) via the tcgen05.cp instruction
- Each 4-bit element in the packed Shared Memory representation is decompressed into a corresponding 8-bit element in Tensor Memory
- The color coding is consistent between source and destination: matching colors in both bars represent the same logical data elements, demonstrating that the values are preserved but stored in a wider format
- The packed source (.b4x16_p64) has elements stored compactly; the destination (.b8x16) has each element zero-extended or sign-extended to 8 bits
- The overall data pattern (color sequence) is maintained across the decompression, confirming element-to-element correspondence

TEXT LABELS SUMMARY:
- "Shared Memory" — identifies the source memory type
- ".b4x16_p64" — identifies the source data format (4-bit elements, 16 per tile, 64-bit packed)
- "tcgen05.cp" — the PTX instruction performing the decompression copy
- "Tensor Memory" — identifies the destination memory type
- ".b8x16" — identifies the destination data format (8-bit elements, 16 per tile)
tcgen05-decompression-4b8b-individual.pn:
Individual Decompression from 4-bit to 8-bit

CONCEPT:
This figure illustrates how a single 4-bit floating point value is decompressed/expanded into an 8-bit floating point value. It shows the bit-field mapping between a compact 4-bit representation and its expanded 8-bit form.

TOP ROW - 8-bit Source Register (Input):
A row of 8 individual bit cells numbered from right to left: 7, 6, 5, 4, 3, 2, 1, 0
These represent bit positions 0 through 7 of an 8-bit value.
All cells are shown as plain white/empty boxes.

MIDDLE SECTION - 4-bit Field Extraction:
Three labeled fields are extracted from specific bit positions of the 8-bit input:
- S (Sign bit): shown in green/light green background, occupies 1 bit
- E2 (Exponent field): shown in yellow/light yellow background, occupies 2 bits
- M1 (Mantissa field): shown in orange/light orange background, occupies 1 bit

These three fields together form the 4-bit compressed representation (1 + 2 + 1 = 4 bits total).

ARROW:
A large solid blue downward-pointing arrow indicates the decompression transformation direction, from the 4-bit extracted fields to the 8-bit output.

BOTTOM ROW - 8-bit Output (Decompressed Result):
A row of 8 bit cells representing the expanded 8-bit value, laid out from left to right:
- Bit 7: 0 (plain white, zero-filled)
- Bit 6: 0 (plain white, zero-filled)
- Bit 5: S (green background, sign bit placed here)
- Bit 4-3: E2 (yellow background, exponent bits placed here)
- Bit 2: M1 (orange background, mantissa bit placed here)
- Bit 1: 0 (plain white, zero-filled)
- Bit 0: 0 (plain white, zero-filled)

DATA FLOW / MAPPING PATTERN:
The 4-bit fields (S, E2, M1) are extracted from the input 8-bit register and then repositioned within an 8-bit output word. The upper 2 bits (positions 7-6) are zero-filled, the lower 2 bits (positions 1-0) are zero-filled, and the S, E2, M1 fields are placed in the middle bit positions (5, 4-3, 2) of the 8-bit output. This represents a format conversion where the compact 4-bit float fields are mapped into corresponding fields of an 8-bit float format with zero padding on both ends.
tcgen05-decompression-6b8b.png:
CONCEPT BEING ILLUSTRATED:
This figure illustrates the decompression operation from 6-bit packed data format to 8-bit data format, specifically showing how the tcgen05.cp instruction copies and decompresses data from Shared Memory into Tensor Memory on NVIDIA GPUs.

OVERALL LAYOUT:
The figure contains two horizontal memory layout bars arranged vertically, with a downward arrow between them indicating the direction of data flow (copy/decompression operation).

TOP BAR - SHARED MEMORY:
Label: "Shared Memory" (positioned at top-left)
Format label: ".b6x16_p32" (positioned above the bar, roughly centered)
Description: A horizontal bar representing a region of shared memory containing 6-bit packed data. The bar is visually shorter/narrower than the Tensor Memory bar below it, reflecting that 6-bit data is more compact. The bar contains a repeating pattern of colored segments (green, blue/light blue, yellow/tan, pink/salmon, and white segments) separated by thin black dividers. The colored segments appear in groups, representing packed 6-bit values. The rightmost portion of the shared memory bar contains predominantly white/empty-looking segments, which likely represent padding bits associated with the .p32 (pad to 32-bit) format.

ARROW:
A solid blue downward-pointing arrow is positioned between the two bars, centered horizontally.
Label: "tcgen05.cp" (positioned to the right of the arrow)
This indicates the PTX instruction responsible for performing the copy and decompression operation.

BOTTOM BAR - TENSOR MEMORY:
Label: "Tensor Memory" (positioned at bottom-left)
Format label: ".b8x16" (positioned below the bar, roughly centered)
Description: A horizontal bar representing a region of tensor memory containing 8-bit decompressed data. This bar is visually longer/wider than the Shared Memory bar above it, reflecting that 8-bit data occupies more space than 6-bit data. The bar contains a similar repeating pattern of colored segments (green, blue/light blue, yellow/tan, pink/salmon) separated by thin black dividers. The pattern extends further to the right compared to the shared memory bar, and notably there are no white/padding segments — all segments appear to contain valid colored data values.

COLOR PATTERN AND DATA ORGANIZATION:
Both bars use the same color coding to indicate corresponding data elements:
- Green segments
- Light blue/periwinkle segments
- Yellow/tan/gold segments
- Pink/salmon segments
These colors repeat in a consistent cyclic pattern across both bars, allowing visual correspondence between the compressed source data and the decompressed destination data.

NUMERICAL/FORMAT INFORMATION:
- Source format: .b6x16_p32 — meaning 16 elements of 6-bit values, packed with 32-bit padding
- Destination format: .b8x16 — meaning 16 elements of 8-bit values
- Decompression ratio: 6-bit to 8-bit (each 6-bit value is expanded to an 8-bit value)
- The 16 data elements are preserved; only the bit-width per element changes (6 to 8 bits)

DATA FLOW SUMMARY:
Shared Memory (.b6x16_p32) --> [tcgen05.cp instruction] --> Tensor Memory (.b8x16)
The 6-bit packed values (with padding) in shared memory are decompressed/unpacked into full 8-bit values stored in tensor memory. The tensor memory bar is wider than the shared memory bar, visually confirming the expansion in data size from 6-bit to 8-bit representation.
tcgen05-decompression-6b8b-individual1.p:
This figure illustrates the individual decompression process from a 6-bit E3M2 floating-point format to an 8-bit format.

CONCEPT:
The figure shows how a 6-bit E3M2 floating-point value is expanded/decompressed into an 8-bit representation by zero-extending the upper bits.

LAYOUT DESCRIPTION:

Row 1 - Bit Position Header:
A row of boxes labeled with bit positions from left to right: 7, 6, 5, 4, 3, 2, 1, 0
These represent the 8 bit positions of the output byte.

Row 2 - 6-bit E3M2 Source Format:
This row is offset to the right, covering only bit positions 5 down to 0 (6 bits total).
The fields are:
- S (Sign bit): occupies bit position 5, colored green/yellow
- E3 (3-bit Exponent): occupies bit positions 4, 3, 2, colored blue/teal
- M2 (2-bit Mantissa): occupies bit positions 1, 0, colored yellow/tan

Row 3 - 8-bit Output Format:
This row spans all 8 bit positions (7 down to 0).
The fields are:
- Bit 7: contains value 0 (fixed zero), colored white/gray
- Bit 6: contains value 0 (fixed zero), colored white/gray
- S (Sign bit): occupies bit position 5, colored green/yellow — same as source
- E3 (3-bit Exponent): occupies bit positions 4, 3, 2, colored blue/teal — same as source
- M2 (2-bit Mantissa): occupies bit positions 1, 0, colored yellow/tan — same as source

DATA FLOW / PATTERN:
The 6-bit E3M2 value (consisting of 1 sign bit + 3 exponent bits + 2 mantissa bits) is placed into the lower 6 bits (positions 5 through 0) of the 8-bit output. The two most significant bits (positions 7 and 6) of the 8-bit output are filled with zeros (zero-extension). The internal field ordering and bit assignments of S, E3, and M2 are preserved exactly during decompression.

SUMMARY OF FIELD MAPPING:
Source 6-bit:  [S][E3][E2][E1][M1][M0]  at positions 5-0
Output 8-bit:  [0][0][S][E3][E2][E1][M1][M0]  at positions 7-0
tcgen05-decompression-6b8b-individual2.p:
CONCEPT:
This figure illustrates the bit-level expansion process for decompressing a 6-bit floating point value (E2M3 format) into an 8-bit floating point value. It shows how the individual bit fields are mapped and zero-extended during decompression.

LAYOUT AND STRUCTURE:

The figure contains three horizontal rows, each representing a bit field layout:

ROW 1 - Bit Position Reference Bar (top row):
A row of 8 numbered boxes showing bit positions from left to right:
7 | 6 | 5 | 4 | 3 | 2 | 1 | 0
These are bit index labels (most significant bit 7 on the left, least significant bit 0 on the right).

ROW 2 - 6-bit Source Format (E2M3, middle row):
This row shows the 6-bit input format aligned to the right portion of the 8-bit field (bits 5 down to 0). It contains three colored fields:
- S (Sign bit): 1 bit wide, green/teal background, aligned at bit position 5
- E2 (Exponent, 2 bits): blue/purple background, spanning bit positions 4 and 3
- M3 (Mantissa, 3 bits): yellow/tan background, spanning bit positions 2, 1, and 0

The 6-bit source occupies positions 5 through 0 (the rightmost 6 positions of the 8-bit reference bar). Positions 7 and 6 are not covered by this row (the row starts at bit 5).

ROW 3 - 8-bit Destination Format (bottom row):
This row shows the full 8-bit output format. It contains:
- 0: a zero-filled bit at position 7 (white/empty background)
- 0: a zero-filled bit at position 6 (white/empty background)
- S (Sign bit): 1 bit wide, green/teal background, at bit position 5
- E2 (Exponent, 2 bits): blue/purple background, spanning bit positions 4 and 3
- M3 (Mantissa, 3 bits): yellow/tan background, spanning bit positions 2, 1, and 0

DATA FLOW AND MAPPING:
- The 6-bit E2M3 source value occupies bits [5:0] of the 8-bit output.
- The two most significant bits (positions 7 and 6) of the 8-bit output are filled with zeros (zero-extension).
- The Sign (S), Exponent (E2), and Mantissa (M3) fields are preserved in their relative positions during decompression.
- No bit reordering occurs; the fields map directly from the 6-bit source to the lower 6 bits of the 8-bit destination.

FIELD SUMMARY:
Field | Bits in source | Bits in destination | Color
S     | 1 bit (bit 5)  | 1 bit (bit 5)       | Green/teal
E2    | 2 bits (4-3)   | 2 bits (4-3)        | Blue/purple
M3    | 3 bits (2-0)   | 3 bits (2-0)        | Yellow/tan
Zero  | N/A            | 2 bits (7-6)        | White (value 0)
tcgen05-packing-formats-mxf8f6f4-tmem-di:
4-BIT PACKING FORMAT WITH TYPE E2M1

SOURCE DATA TYPE:
- Type: E2M1 (floating point with 2 exponent bits and 1 mantissa bit)
- Bit width: 4 bits per element (1 sign bit + 2 exponent bits + 1 mantissa bit = 4 bits)

CONTAINER:
- Container size: 8 bits (1 byte)
- Bit positions labeled 7 (MSB) down to 0 (LSB)

BIT-LEVEL LAYOUT WITHIN THE 8-BIT CONTAINER:

Bit 7: 0 (padding, fixed zero)
Bit 6: 0 (padding, fixed zero)
Bit 5: S (sign bit of E2M1 element)
Bit 4: E2 (exponent bit, high)
Bit 3: E2 (exponent bit, low)
Bit 2: M1 (mantissa bit)
Bit 1: 0 (padding, fixed zero)
Bit 0: 0 (padding, fixed zero)

FIELD SUMMARY TABLE:
Bits 7-6  | Value: 00       | Role: Upper padding (2 bits, fixed zero)
Bit  5    | Value: S        | Role: Sign bit (green shading)
Bits 4-3  | Value: E2       | Role: 2-bit exponent (blue shading)
Bit  2    | Value: M1       | Role: 1-bit mantissa (yellow/orange shading)
Bits 1-0  | Value: 00       | Role: Lower padding (2 bits, fixed zero)

PACKING DETAILS:
- Only 1 element of E2M1 is stored per byte
- The 4-bit E2M1 value occupies bits 5:2 (centered within the byte)
- 4 padding bits total: 2 upper bits (7:6) and 2 lower bits (1:0), all set to zero
- This is NOT a dense packing; the element is not packed at the LSB or MSB boundary

CONTEXT:
- No explicit memory type (shared/tensor/register) is labeled in the figure
- The figure shows the internal bit field decomposition of a single E2M1 value within an 8-bit container

6-BIT PACKING FORMAT WITH TYPE E3M2

SOURCE DATA TYPE
Type: E3M2 (floating point with 3 exponent bits and 2 mantissa bits)
Bit width per element: 6 bits (1 sign bit + 3 exponent bits + 2 mantissa bits)

CONTAINER
Container size: 8 bits (1 byte)
Bit positions shown: 7, 6, 5, 4, 3, 2, 1, 0 (standard byte, MSB to LSB)

BIT-LEVEL LAYOUT WITHIN THE 8-BIT CONTAINER

Bit 7: 0 (padding, fixed zero, shown in white)
Bit 6: 0 (padding, fixed zero, shown in white)
Bit 5: S (sign bit, shown in green)
Bits 4-2: E3 (3-bit exponent field, shown in blue, spanning bits 4, 3, 2)
Bits 1-0: M2 (2-mantissa field, shown in yellow/orange, spanning bits 1, 0)

FIELD SUMMARY TABLE
Bit Position | Field | Description
7            | 0     | Padding (fixed zero)
6            | 0     | Padding (fixed zero)
5            | S     | Sign bit
4            | E3    | Exponent bit 2 (MSB of exponent)
3            | E3    | Exponent bit 1
2            | E3    | Exponent bit 0 (LSB of exponent)
1            | M2    | Mantissa bit 1 (MSB of mantissa)
0            | M2    | Mantissa bit 0 (LSB of mantissa)

PADDING
2 padding bits occupy the most significant positions (bits 7 and 6), both set to 0.
This means a 6-bit E3M2 value is stored in the lower 6 bits of an 8-bit byte, with 2 zero-padding bits at the top.

CONTEXT
The figure illustrates a single-element packing scheme where one E3M2 element occupies bits 5:0 of a byte, with bits 7:6 unused (zeroed). No context for shared memory, tensor memory, or registers is explicitly shown in this figure; it depicts the bit-field decomposition of the format itself.

6-BIT PACKING FORMAT WITH TYPE E2M3

SOURCE DATA TYPE:
- Type: E2M3 (floating point with 2 exponent bits and 3 mantissa bits)
- Bit width per element: 6 bits total (1 sign bit + 2 exponent bits + 3 mantissa bits)

CONTAINER:
- Container size: 8 bits (1 byte)
- The figure shows a single byte with bit positions labeled 7 down to 0 (left to right)

BIT-LEVEL LAYOUT WITHIN THE BYTE:

Bit position 7: 0 (padding, fixed zero)
Bit position 6: 0 (padding, fixed zero)
Bit position 5: S (sign bit of E2M3 element)
Bit position 4: E2 high bit (exponent bit 1)
Bit position 3: E2 low bit (exponent bit 0)
Bit position 2: M3 high bit (mantissa bit 2)
Bit position 1: M3 mid bit (mantissa bit 1)
Bit position 0: M3 low bit (mantissa bit 0)

FIELD SUMMARY TABLE:
Bits [7:6] = 00 (two padding bits, fixed zero, shown in white)
Bit  [5]   = S  (sign bit, shown in green)
Bits [4:3] = E2 (2-bit exponent, shown in blue/purple)
Bits [2:0] = M3 (3-bit mantissa, shown in yellow/tan)

PADDING:
- 2 padding bits at the most significant positions (bits 7 and 6)
- Both padding bits are explicitly shown as 0

PACKING DENSITY:
- One E2M3 element per byte
- 2 bits wasted per byte as zero padding
- Effective utilization: 6 of 8 bits

CONTEXT:
- This describes how a single 6-bit E2M3 value is stored/packed into an 8-bit byte container
- The element is right-justified with zero padding in the upper 2 bits
tcgen05-packing-formats-mxf8f6f4-smem-di:
4-BIT PACKING FORMAT - COMPLETE EXTRACTION

FIGURE TITLE/CAPTION: "4-bit packing format"

---

1. SOURCE DATA TYPE AND BIT WIDTH

Data type: E2M1 (4-bit floating point)
Bit width per element: 4 bits
Format family: MX (Microscaling) floating point
Specific format name as used in instruction: mxf8f6f4 kind with E2M1 matrix element type

---

2. MEMORY TARGET

This format is for SHARED MEMORY (explicitly labeled "Shared Memory" on the left side of the figure).

---

3. PACKING STRUCTURE

Number of data elements per row/group: 16 elements of 4-bit floating point type
Total data bits for 16 elements: 16 x 4 = 64 bits
Padding appended: 64-bit padding added at the end of the 16 elements
Total container width: 64 bits (data) + 64 bits (padding) = 128 bits total per row

The shared memory row shown in the figure contains a wide horizontal strip of small cells. The first half (left portion) contains the 16 colored/shaded 4-bit elements, and the second half (right portion) consists of unshaded/white cells representing the 64-bit padding.

---

4. BIT POSITIONS OF ELEMENTS

Each element occupies 4 bits.
Element 0: bits [3:0]
Element 1: bits [7:4]
Element 2: bits [11:8]
Element 3: bits [15:12]
Element 4: bits [19:16]
Element 5: bits [23:20]
Element 6: bits [27:24]
Element 7: bits [31:28]
Element 8: bits [35:32]
Element 9: bits [39:36]
Element 10: bits [43:40]
Element 11: bits [47:44]
Element 12: bits [51:48]
Element 13: bits [55:52]
Element 14: bits [59:56]
Element 15: bits [63:60]

---

5. PADDING BITS AND POSITIONS

Padding type: 64-bit zero/unused padding
Padding position: bits [127:64] — immediately following the 16 data elements
Padding purpose: Alignment padding appended at the end of the 16-element group
The figure explicitly states: "16 elements of 4-bit floating point type, with 64-bit padding at the end"

---

6. INSTRUCTION CONTEXT

PTX instruction: tcgen05.mma.kind::mxf8f6f4
Matrix element type specified in instruction descriptor: E2M1
This packing format is consumed by the tcgen05.mma tensor core generation 5 matrix multiply-accumulate instruction operating on MX E2M1 (2-bit exponent, 1-bit mantissa) 4-bit floating point data stored in shared memory.

6-BIT PACKING FORMAT - COMPLETE EXTRACTION

1. SOURCE DATA TYPE AND BIT WIDTH
   - Data type: 6-bit floating point
   - Supported formats: E3M2 (1 sign bit, 3 exponent bits, 2 mantissa bits) or E2M3 (1 sign bit, 2 exponent bits, 3 mantissa bits)
   - Each element is exactly 6 bits wide

2. CONTAINER AND ELEMENT COUNT
   - Memory type: Shared Memory
   - 16 elements of 6-bit floating point type are packed per group
   - 16 elements x 6 bits = 96 bits of actual data
   - 32-bit padding is appended at the end of the 16-element group
   - Total container size: 96 bits (data) + 32 bits (padding) = 128 bits (16 bytes) per group of 16 elements

3. BIT POSITIONS OF ELEMENTS
   - Elements are packed contiguously, back-to-back, with no gaps between individual elements
   - Element 0: bits 0-5
   - Element 1: bits 6-11
   - Element 2: bits 12-17
   - Element 3: bits 18-23
   - Element 4: bits 24-29
   - Element 5: bits 30-35
   - Element 6: bits 36-41
   - Element 7: bits 42-47
   - Element 8: bits 48-53
   - Element 9: bits 54-59
   - Element 10: bits 60-65
   - Element 11: bits 66-71
   - Element 12: bits 72-77
   - Element 13: bits 78-83
   - Element 14: bits 84-89
   - Element 15: bits 90-95

4. PADDING BITS AND POSITIONS
   - 32-bit padding appended at the END of the 16-element block
   - Padding occupies bits 96-127 (the final 32 bits of the 128-bit container)
   - Padding bits are shown as white/empty cells in the figure at the rightmost portion of the memory strip

5. MEMORY TYPE
   - This format is for SHARED MEMORY (explicitly labeled "Shared Memory" in the figure)
   - The instruction that consumes this format is: tcgen05.mma.kind::mxf8f6f4
   - The matrix element type (E3M2 or E2M3) is specified in the instruction descriptor
   - The kind qualifier mxf8f6f4 indicates this instruction handles mixed 8-bit, 6-bit, and 4-bit floating point formats

SUMMARY TABLE
Field                  Value
Data element width     6 bits
Elements per group     16
Data bits per group    96 bits
Padding per group      32 bits (at end)
Total group size       128 bits (16 bytes)
Packing style         Contiguous, no inter-element padding
Memory target         Shared Memory
Instruction           tcgen05.mma.kind::mxf8f6f4
Supported types       E3M2, E2M3
tcgen05-packing-formats-mxf4-tmem-dig1.p:
4-BIT PACKING FORMAT WITH TYPE E2M1

SOURCE DATA TYPE:
- Type: E2M1 (floating point with 1 sign bit, 2 exponent bits, 1 mantissa bit)
- Bit width per element: 4 bits total (1 sign + 2 exponent + 1 mantissa)

CONTAINER:
- Container size: 8 bits (1 byte)
- Number of elements packed per byte: 2 elements

BIT-LEVEL LAYOUT WITHIN 8-BIT CONTAINER:
Bit positions are labeled 7 down to 0 (MSB to LSB).

Upper element (bits 7:4):
- Bit 7: S (sign bit)
- Bits 6-5: E2 (2 exponent bits)
- Bit 4: M1 (1 mantissa bit)

Lower element (bits 3:0):
- Bit 3: S (sign bit)
- Bits 2-1: E2 (2 exponent bits)
- Bit 0: M1 (1 mantissa bit)

ELEMENT FIELD WIDTHS:
- S field: 1 bit
- E2 field: 2 bits
- M1 field: 1 bit
- Total per element: 4 bits

PADDING:
- No padding bits present; all 8 bits are used by the two E2M1 elements

PACKING ORDER:
- Element 0 (lower) occupies bits 3:0
- Element 1 (upper) occupies bits 7:4
- Elements are packed contiguously with no gaps

CONTEXT:
- No explicit memory type (shared, tensor, or register) is labeled in the figure; the figure illustrates the generic byte-level packing format for E2M1 sub-byte data type

  - B columns (N/2) to N-1:    warp-rank % 4 == 3  +  Odd CTA in CTA-Pair    [light yellow background]

COLOR CODING SUMMARY:
- Dark green: warp-rank % 4 == 0, Even CTA
- Medium/light green: warp-rank % 4 == 1 or 2, Even CTA

- Dark gold/orange: warp-rank % 4 == 0, Odd CTA

4. Warp-rank modulo 4 determines which 32-row band and which N-half a warp covers.
5. Warp-rank % 4 values 0 and 1 cover the left B-column half; values 2 and 3 cover the right B-column half — interleaved across the row bands.
6. The pattern assigns warp-rank % 4 == 0 and 2 to even M-bands (0-31, 64-95) and warp-rank % 4 == 1 and 3 to odd M-bands (32-63, 96-127).

  Color: Dark green

- Row 1: "warp-rank % 4 == 0"
- Row 2: "warp-rank % 4 == 1"

- Row 1: "warp-rank % 4 == 2"

WARP-RANK ASSIGNMENT MAPPING:

A Matrix rows 0-31  + B Matrix columns 0 to (N/2)-1   → warp-rank % 4 == 0

- M dimension (64 rows) is split into two halves: rows 0-31 and rows 32-63
- N dimension is split into two halves: columns 0 to (N/2)-1 and (N/2) to N-1
- Four warp-ranks (identified by warp-rank modulo 4: values 0, 1, 2, 3) are assigned to the four quadrants
- Warp-ranks 0 and 1 handle the left N half (columns 0 to (N/2)-1), for upper and lower M halves respectively
- Warp-ranks 2 and 3 handle the right N half (columns (N/2) to N-1), for upper and lower M halves respectively
- The assignment increments by 1 going down the M dimension (0→1, 2→3) and by 2 going across the N dimension (0→2, 1→3)
